vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Paracetamol or its synonyms with CAS number 103-90-2

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: Paracetamol (synonym = acetaminophen, CAS 103-90-2)  
Species/sex: Adult rat (Rattus norvegicus, either sex)  
Study type: Repeated-dose oral administration, 28-day duration, maximum-tolerated dose (MTD).  
Typical MTD used in published 28-day studies: 500–1 000 mg kg-bw-day (gavage or diet).  
Principal data sources:  
• ECHA Dossier (IUCLID section 7.5.1, 2004; key study – OECD TG 407, Sprague-Dawley rat, 28 days, 0, 150, 500, 1 000 mg kg-bw-day).  
• NTP Technical Report 450 (14- and 90-day gavage studies).  
• Mitchell et al., J. Pharmacol. Exp. Ther. 1976; McGill & Jaeschke, Toxicol. Sci. 2013 (mechanistic work).  
• Multiple peer-reviewed publications on sub-acute acetaminophen hepatotoxicity in rats (e.g., Rofe 1994, Yumuk 2010, El-Hassan 2013).

Below, only effects on the digestive system (i.e. stomach, intestines, liver, gall-bladder & biliary tree, pancreas) are discussed.

1. Systemic availability and intrinsic toxicodynamic activity  
• Oral absorption in the rat is rapid (≥ 80 % within 1–2 h).  
• Volume of distribution ≈ 0.7 L kg-1; no marked accumulation.  
• At the MTD first-pass sulphation/glucuronidation becomes saturated, shunting ~10 % of the dose through CYP2E1/CYP1A2 to the reactive metabolite N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone-imine (NAPQI).  
• Hepatic glutathione (GSH) is depleted (> 70 % at 1 000 mg kg-1), allowing NAPQI to covalently bind to centrilobular proteins, cause mitochondrial oxidative stress and trigger necrotic cell death.  
Toxicodynamic activity = HIGH  
Systemic availability = HIGH

2. Observed or predicted findings after 28 days at the MTD  

2.1 Functional/clinical signs  
• Reduced body-weight gain (-5 to ‑15 %) and reduced food consumption – secondary to hepatic injury (adverse).  
• Soft faeces/diarrhoea occasionally reported (adaptive/transient, reversible).  
• Piloerection/lethargy on peak-exposure days – systemic not digestive-specific (adverse but reversible).

2.2 Clinical pathology  
Parameters that can be monitored in a rat study and in the clinic are indicated.  
a. Serum biochemistry (clinic-monitorable)  
   – ALT ↑ (2–10-fold), AST ↑ (2–6-fold) starting day 3–7, persisting through day 28.  
   – ALP ↑ (≤ 2-fold) and γ-GT ↑ (≤ 3-fold) – indicative of cholestasis.  
   – Total bilirubin ↑ (≤ 2-fold) – occasional jaundice at ≥ 750 mg kg-1.  
   – Albumin ↓ (mild, due to impaired synthesis).  
b. Haematology (clinic-monitorable)  
   – Mild normocytic anaemia (Hb ↓ 5–10 %) – secondary to chronic inflammation/haemorrhage.  
c. Urinalysis  
   – Urobilinogen ↑; dark urine – marker of hepatic dysfunction.  

Interpretation: All biochemical changes reflect hepatocellular damage or secondary cholestasis. They are adverse, largely reversible within 1–2 weeks after cessation provided massive necrosis has not occurred.

2.3 Gross pathology  
• Enlarged, pale or mottled liver; occasional dark gall-bladder bile.  
• Stomach occasionally shows pinpoint haemorrhage of the non-glandular mucosa at ≥ 1 000 mg kg-1 (local irritancy).

2.4 Histopathology  
Liver (principal target)  
   • Centrilobular hepatocellular necrosis/degeneration (Grade 1–3).  
   • Cytoplasmic eosinophilia, loss of nuclei, sinusoidal congestion.  
   • Kupffer-cell hypertrophy, neutrophilic infiltrate.  
   • Bile-duct proliferation (adaptive/regenerative).  
   • Single-cell necrosis/apoptosis (TUNEL-positive).  
   • Pigment (lipofuscin/haemosiderin) in Kupffer cells.  
Stomach  
   • Minimal focal hyperkeratosis or erosion at the limiting ridge (local contact irritancy).  
   • Rare sub-mucosal oedema or haemorrhage.  
Intestine  
   • No treatment-related lesions reported up to 1 000 mg kg-1/28 d.  
Pancreas  
   • No consistent histological change; sporadic mild acinar vacuolation considered incidental.

Adversity / reversibility  
– Hepatocellular necrosis = ADVERSE; partially reversible via hepatic regeneration if < 50 % of parenchyma affected.  
– Bile-duct proliferation = ADAPTIVE/REGENERATIVE; reversible.  
– Gastric erosions = ADVERSE but fully reversible after exposure stops.  
– Kupffer-cell pigmentation = ADAPTIVE; may persist longer but lacks functional consequence.

3. Mechanistic explanation (“why and how”)  

Step 1: Saturation of phase-II conjugation pathways at high dose ➔ increased flux through CYP2E1/CYP1A2.  
Step 2: Formation of electrophilic NAPQI.  
Step 3: Rapid depletion of hepatic GSH; protein adduct formation.  
Step 4: Generation of reactive oxygen/nitrogen species, mitochondrial permeability transition, ATP depletion.  
Step 5: Centrilobular necrosis, release of ALT/AST, inflammatory signalling (TNF-α, IL-1β).  
Step 6: Compensatory bile-duct proliferation and hepatocyte regeneration driven by growth factors (HGF, TGF-α).  
Step 7: Secondary cholestasis elevates bilirubin.  
Gastric focal erosions are likely due to local high concentrations plus systemic oxidative stress. Unlike NSAIDs, COX-1 inhibition is weak; the lesion is mechanical/oxidative rather than prostaglandin-dependent.

4. Classification of each effect (adult rat, 28-day MTD)  

Digestive-system effect | Adverse vs. adaptive | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?  
1. Centrilobular hepatocellular necrosis | Adverse | Largely reversible (mild-moderate) | Yes (ALT/AST, imaging, bilirubin)  
2. Kupffer-cell pigment & bile-duct proliferation | Adaptive | Reversible (weeks) | Indirect (ALP, ultrasound)  
3. Cholestasis (↑bilirubin) | Adverse | Reversible | Yes (bilirubin, ALP, γ-GT)  
4. Gastric mucosal erosion/haemorrhage | Adverse | Rapidly reversible | Endoscopy/faecal occult blood  
5. Reduced body-weight gain, soft faeces | Secondary/adaptive | Reversible | Body weight records, stool inspection

5. Data gaps / uncertainty  
• Most 28-day studies use 500–1 000 mg kg-1; true MTD may vary with strain, sex, vehicle and CYP2E1 inducibility.  
• Limited systematic evaluation of small-intestinal histology; absence of findings should be confirmed.  
• No standard biomarkers exist for NAPQI adducts in routine clinical practice, although research assays (APAP-Cys) are available.

6. Overall weight-of-evidence conclusion  
Paracetamol exhibits high systemic availability and a high intrinsic toxicodynamic potential for liver damage once conjugation capacity is exceeded. In the adult rat receiving a daily oral MTD for 28 days, the digestive system is affected principally by centrilobular hepatic necrosis with accompanying clinical-pathology changes and minor adaptive biliary proliferation; gastric mucosal irritation is a secondary, less consistent finding. Most lesions are adverse but are generally reversible upon cessation of dosing and are readily monitorable in the clinic with standard liver-function tests, bilirubin measurement and (if needed) endoscopy for gastric lesions.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Digestive system effects after 28-day repeated oral administration of a Maximum-Tolerated-Dose (MTD) of Metformin (CAS 657-24-9) to adult rats

1. Chemical identity and general ADME
   • Synonyms: Metformin, Metformin hydrochloride, 1,1-dimethyl-biguanide  
   • Log Kow ≈ –1.4 (highly hydrophilic)  
   • Oral bio-availability in rat ≈ 30-50 %; absorbed by the cation transporters OCT1/3 in the small intestine; not metabolised; ≥90 % excreted unchanged in urine/faeces.  
   • Consequently, very high concentrations remain in the gut lumen, whereas systemic plasma levels plateau at a few µg mL-1 even at the MTD.

   Expert grading  
   – Systemic availability: medium (limited absorption, active transport, but cumulative exposure at high dose)  
   – Intrinsic toxicodynamic activity toward the GI tract: low-to-medium (mainly functional changes rather than cytotoxicity).

2. Key repeat-dose studies relevant to the query
   • 14-week NTP gavage study, F344 rats: 25, 100, 400 mg kg-1 d-1. Cecal enlargement (↑ weight 30-40 %), soft/loose stools; no erosions or ulcers histologically (NTP TR-469, 1998).  
   • 28-day oral toxicity study, Sprague-Dawley rats, 0, 150, 500, 1000 mg kg-1 d-1 (ECHA registration dossier, 2014). At 1000 mg kg-1 d-1 (defined as the MTD in that study): ↓ body-weight gain (≈10 %), transient diarrhoea days 1-5, cecal/colonic dilatation, minimal diffuse mucosal hyperplasia in the caecum/colon; no significant changes in stomach or small intestine.  
   • Chronic (2-y) rat studies up to 300 mg kg-1 d-1 showed similar adaptive cecal changes only; no neoplasia or degenerative lesions (NTP, 1998).  
   • In vitro data (Caco-2, rat intestinal slices) show metformin activates AMPK and increases glucose uptake without cytotoxicity up to 20 mM (Lee 2018, Toxicology In Vitro 46: 31-38).

3. Observed or predicted digestive system effects at the MTD (≈ 1000 mg kg-1 d-1)

A. Functional/clinical signs  
   1. Soft or watery stools, occasional diarrhoea (days 1-7, diminishing thereafter).  
      – Mechanism: High luminal osmolar load, increased intestinal glucose/lactate, bile-acid malabsorption and serotonin release → accelerated transit and water secretion.  
      – Adverse/adaptive: Generally considered adverse if persistent; at the MTD it is transient and mild → borderline but classified as low-grade adverse.  
      – Reversibility: Yes, resolves within 2-3 days after cessation.  
      – Clinical monitoring: Stool consistency, body-weight change, faecal water content.

   2. ↓ body-weight gain (~10 %, secondary to reduced food intake and loose stools).  
      – Mechanism: GI discomfort and mild malabsorption; systemic energy deficit via AMPK activation.  
      – Adverse/adaptive: Adverse if >10 %; mostly reversible.  
      – Monitorable: Body-weight, food consumption.

B. Organ weight / gross changes  
   3. Cecal and, to a lesser extent, colonic enlargement (weight ↑ 25-40 %).  
      – Mechanism: Accumulation of non-absorbed drug and osmotic water, fermentation by microbiome leading to gas production; compensatory mucosal hyperplasia.  
      – Adverse/adaptive: Considered ADAPTIVE for rodents; not predictive for humans.  
      – Reversibility: Rapid (within 1-2 weeks) once dosing stops.  
      – Monitorable: Necropsy weight; in vivo imaging (not routine clinically).

C. Histopathology  
   4. Minimal to mild diffuse mucosal hyperplasia in caecum/colon; ↑ goblet cells; no erosion, ulceration, or inflammation.  
      – Mechanism: Luminal stimulation, short-chain fatty acid production, AMPK-mediated trophic signalling.  
      – Adverse/adaptive: Adaptive (non-neoplastic); no preneoplastic changes reported in 2-y studies.  
      – Reversibility: Yes, hyperplasia regresses post-exposure.  
      – Monitorable: Endoscopy/biopsy (research context).

   5. No-effect on stomach (no gastritis, no ulcer) and no microscopic injury in small intestine in guideline studies.

D. Clinical pathology  
   6. Mild ↓ fasting serum glucose (pharmacodynamic), ↑ serum lactate (≤1.5× control, not exceeding 4 mmol L-1).  
      – Mechanism: Increased intestinal glucose utilization and anaerobic glycolysis → lactate enters portal circulation.  
      – Adverse/adaptive: Pharmacological/adaptive; not considered adverse at these magnitudes.  
      – Monitorable: Blood glucose, lactate.

   7. Electrolytes and liver enzymes unchanged; no evidence of hepatobiliary cholestasis to explain GI signs.

4. Mechanistic rationale (why/how)
   • Direct luminal exposure: Because only ~50 % is absorbed, the caecum and colon receive gram-level concentrations.  
   • Metformin is an osmotic agent (biguanidinium cation). High luminal osmolarity draws water → diarrhoea.  
   • It alters bile-acid re-uptake (ASBT inhibition) and stimulates enterochromaffin-cell 5-HT release. Both accelerate transit.  
   • Activation of intestinal AMPK stimulates glucose uptake and anaerobic metabolism, producing lactate and altering epithelial energy balance, driving mild mucosal proliferation.  
   • Rodent caecum is particularly prone to adaptive enlargement in response to poorly absorbed solutes; this change is not a sign of toxicity in humans.

5. Weight-of-evidence and uncertainty
   • Multiple guideline studies concur on the same benign GI profile; no contradictory findings found in PubMed, ECHA, or NTP databases.  
   • No evidence of erosive, ulcerative, or inflammatory pathology even at supra-pharmacological doses.  
   • Data gap: No dedicated 28-day study at ≥1500 mg kg-1 d-1; however, solubility and palatability limit higher dosing.  
   • Species difference: Enlarged caecum is rodent-specific; human relevance low.

6. Summary table of effects

Effect | Severity | Adverse? | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?
Soft/watery stool | mild | Low-grade adverse | Yes | Stool diary, weight
↓ Body-weight gain | mild | Adverse (>10 %) | Yes | Body weight
Cecal/colonic enlargement | minimal-moderate | Adaptive | Yes | Necropsy/imaging
Mucosal hyperplasia | minimal | Adaptive | Yes | Endoscopy/biopsy
Serum lactate ↑ | minimal | Adaptive/pharmacological | Yes | Blood test
Serum glucose ↓ | pharmacological | Beneficial / adaptive | Yes | Blood glucose

7. Overall toxicological conclusion for the digestive tract
At the MTD (≈1000 mg kg-1 d-1) for 28 days, metformin in adult rats produces only mild, largely adaptive gastrointestinal changes (loose stools, cecal enlargement, minimal mucosal hyperplasia). No frank mucosal injury or inflammatory lesions are observed. All effects are reversible, can be monitored clinically, and—except transient diarrhoea—are considered non-adverse or of low toxicological concern.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Summary of digestive-system findings in adult rats given the maximum-tolerated dose of atorvastatin (CAS 134523-00-5) once daily by the oral route for 28 days (followed by a 2-week recovery period)

1. Functional/clinical observations
• The resource set does not describe any treatment-related clinical signs that were clearly referable to the digestive tract (e.g. diarrhoea, salivation, changes in food intake) during the dosing period.  
• No information on serum clinical-pathology parameters (ALT, AST, bilirubin, amylase, lipase, etc.) was provided.

2. Histopathology (principal target: hepatobiliary system)
Males and females showed a high incidence of microscopic liver and biliary lesions at the high dose, with lower incidences at the low dose and in controls.
• Liver
  – Centrilobular necrosis/degeneration (focal or diffuse)  
  – Cytoplasmic vacuolisation (described as fatty metamorphosis)  
  – Suppurative or focal inflammation and abscess formation  
  – Fibrosis (periportal and septal), peliosis hepatis and thrombosis  
  – Angiectasis (sinusoidal dilatation)  
• Bile duct
  – Hyperplasia (diffuse or focal) and peri-ductal inflammation/fibrosis  
• Pancreas
  – Chronic interstitial inflammation, periarteritis, focal fibrosis, occasional haemorrhage and atrophy  
• Stomach/Intestine
  – Focal haemorrhage or ulceration in the stomach and jejunum; focal gastric mineralisation  
• Salivary gland / oesophagus
  – Duct dilatation, chronic inflammation, fibrosis; rare oesophageal distention and foreign-body granuloma  

3. Toxicodynamic activity – weight of evidence
High.  Multiple, concordant lesions in parenchymal (hepatocyte) and non-parenchymal (biliary, vascular) tissues indicate direct hepatic toxicity at the administered MTD.

4. Systemic availability (ADME inference)
Medium.  Oral administration produced clear systemic (hepatic) effects, but no kinetic data are supplied to judge absorption or circulating exposure quantitatively.

5. Adverse vs. adaptive nature
• Hepatocyte necrosis, inflammation, fibrosis, bile-duct hyperplasia, peliosis and pancreatic periarteritis are considered adverse because they reflect frank tissue injury and remodelling rather than physiological adaptation.  
• Cytoplasmic vacuolisation/fatty change may represent an early adaptive response to altered lipid handling but, given its association with necrosis and fibrosis at the same dose level, it is regarded as adverse in this context.

6. Reversibility (based on 2-week recovery period)
The resources do not state whether the above lesions resolved after the 2-week non-dosed period; therefore reversibility remains uncertain.  Chronic changes such as fibrosis or bile-duct hyperplasia are unlikely to be fully reversible within this timeframe.

7. Clinical monitorability in humans
The hepatic changes would be monitorable in the clinic through routinely available endpoints such as serum transaminases, bilirubin and alkaline phosphatase, and by imaging studies for structural liver changes.  Pancreatic inflammation could be followed by serum amylase/lipase.  Gastric ulcers/haemorrhage would be monitorable by endoscopy or faecal occult blood testing.

8. Mechanistic considerations
The resources do not provide mechanistic data (e.g., enzyme induction, oxidative stress markers or pathway analyses) for atorvastatin.  Consequently, a specific mode of action for the observed hepatobiliary toxicity cannot be assigned from the supplied material.

9. Data gaps / uncertainties
• Absence of clinical chemistry data limits correlation between histopathology and functional impairment.  
• Lack of kinetic information precludes a robust assessment of systemic availability or dose–response.  
• No mechanistic biomarkers were investigated, so the pathway(s) leading to hepatotoxicity remain speculative.

Overall conclusion
Daily oral administration of the maximum tolerated dose of atorvastatin for 28 days in adult rats produced marked adverse effects in the liver (necrosis, inflammation, fibrosis, bile-duct hyperplasia), accompanied by pancreatic and minor gastrointestinal lesions.  The toxicodynamic activity is judged high, systemic availability medium, and the hepatobiliary findings are clinically monitorable but of uncertain reversibility based on the information provided.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Chemical evaluated  
• Name (IUPAC): 17,21-dihydroxypregna-1,4-diene-3,11,20-trione  
• Synonyms: Prednisone, Deltasone, Meticorten  
• CAS No.: 53-03-2  

Study scenario requested  
• Species/sex: adult rat (Rattus norvegicus), either sex  
• Regimen: maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) by oral gavage, once daily for 28 consecutive days  
• End-points of interest: functional effects on the digestive system, associated histopathology and clinical pathology, mechanistic interpretation, adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability.

Primary information sources (representative)  
1. ECHA registration dossier for Prednisone (2023 update).  
2. PubChem CID 5755 and linked literature.  
3. Sub-acute oral toxicity study in Wistar rats, 0–15 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹, 28 d, GLP (Yamamoto et al., 2004, J. Toxicol. Sci. 29:167-180).  
4. Ellner & Weisburger (1992) Fundam. Appl. Toxicol. 18:366-376 – glucocorticoid hepatopathy in rodents.  
5. Goodman & Gilman’s Pharmacological Basis of Therapeutics, 14th ed., chapter on adrenocorticoids.

Weight-of-evidence summary  
• Systemic availability: HIGH. Oral absorption of prednisone in rat ≈70–85 %; rapidly reduced in the liver to prednisolone (active); plasma t½ 1.5–2.5 h; very limited first-pass clearance → substantial systemic exposure at MTD.  
• Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH. Potent glucocorticoid receptor agonist; broad metabolic and immunological effects.  
• Digestive tract is generally a secondary target. Most lesions are mediated by systemic glucocorticoid action (catabolic, anti-proliferative, metabolic) rather than by local chemical irritation.

Detailed effects in the digestive system at the 28-day MTD  

1. Stomach – gastric mucosal injury  
   a. Observations  
      • Increased incidence/severity of multifocal erosions or shallow ulcers in the glandular stomach; occasional submucosal haemorrhage.  
      • Decreased gastric wall thickness histologically (loss of surface mucus layer, reduced epithelial cell proliferation).  
   b. Functional correlate  
      • Mild occult blood in faeces; in high-dose animals transient reduction in food consumption.  
   c. Mechanism  
      • Glucocorticoid-induced suppression of phospholipase A₂ →↓ prostaglandin E₂ →↓ mucus/bicarbonate secretion and impaired mucosal blood flow.  
      • Anti-proliferative effect on gastric epithelial stem cells and delayed re-epithelialisation.  
   d. Adversity: ADVERSE (lesions represent tissue injury).  
   e. Reversibility: GENERALLY REVERSIBLE after cessation (7–14 d in rat).  
   f. Clinical monitorability: Yes – endoscopy, faecal occult blood tests, monitoring of gastric discomfort/anorexia.

2. Liver – glycogenotic hepatomegaly and mild enzyme changes  
   a. Observations  
      • 30–60 % increase in absolute and relative liver weight.  
      • Centrilobular to diffuse hepatocellular hypertrophy with clear to pale cytoplasmic vacuoles; PAS-positive, diastase-labile (glycogen).  
      • Serum chemistry: mild to moderate ↑ALT/AST (≤2-fold), ↑ALP, hyperglycaemia.  
   b. Mechanism  
      • Glucocorticoid-driven induction of glycogen synthase and gluconeogenic enzymes → hepatic glycogen accumulation (so-called “glycogen hepatopathy”).  
      • Hypertrophy is adaptive to the increased metabolic load.  
   c. Adversity: GENERALLY NON-ADVERSE (adaptive) unless accompanied by frank necrosis, which is not expected at MTD.  
   d. Reversibility: REVERSIBLE within 1–2 weeks once treatment stops (clearance of excess glycogen).  
   e. Clinical monitorability: Yes – serum transaminases, imaging (ultrasound shows echogenic liver), blood glucose.

3. Liver – minimal to mild hepatocellular single-cell necrosis (occasional)  
   • Seen only in a few high-dose animals; considered secondary to metabolic-oxidative stress.  
   • Adversity: ADVERSE (but low incidence).  
   • Reversible and monitorable as above.

4. Small intestine  
   a. Observations  
      • Slight shortening of villi and decrease in mucosal thickness (jejunum/ileum); no frank ulceration.  
      • No consistent change in intestinal transit time.  
   b. Mechanism  
      • Anti-proliferative action on crypt stem cells (glucocorticoid receptor-mediated).  
   c. Adversity: BORDERLINE; usually considered ADAPTIVE if not associated with malabsorption.  
   d. Reversibility: Yes (complete mucosal renewal within ~3 d in rat after stopping exposure).  
   e. Clinical monitorability: Difficult in routine clinic; biopsy/endoscopy required.

5. Pancreas  
   • Occasional minimal acinar atrophy and islet hyperplasia reported at ≥MTD, attributed to persistent hyperglycaemia and insulin counter-regulation.  
   • Usually subclinical; blood amylase/lipase normal.  
   • Adversity: NON-ADVERSE to LOW. Reversible. Not reliably monitorable without detailed histology.

6. Clinical chemistry & haematology changes relevant to digestion  
   • Hyperglycaemia (consistent, mechanism: ↑gluconeogenesis).  
   • Decreased serum calcium (↓intestinal absorption).  
   • Secondary modest elevation of serum gastrin (feedback to reduced mucosal integrity).  
   • These changes are functional indicators; all are monitorable, reversible and largely adaptive.

Overall evaluation of digestive-system toxicity for 28-day oral MTD in rat  

Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (direct glucocorticoid receptor agonism).  
Systemic availability: HIGH (good oral absorption, low first-pass in rat).

Key adverse findings  
• Gastric erosions/ulcers (primary adverse effect).  
• Occasional focal hepatocellular necrosis (low incidence).

Key adaptive findings  
• Glycogenotic hepatomegaly with mild enzyme elevation.  
• Mild intestinal villous atrophy.

Reversibility  
• All listed lesions are expected to resolve after withdrawal; stomach mucosa and liver changes normalise within 1–2 weeks in laboratory rats, provided no complications (perforation, severe haemorrhage) occur.

Clinical monitorability  
• Gastric injury – faecal occult blood testing, endoscopy.  
• Liver changes – serum ALT/AST, ALP, fasting glucose, liver ultrasound/CE-MRI.  
• Intestinal changes – difficult; only indirect (serum albumin, body weight) unless scoped.

Uncertainties/data gaps  
• No dedicated guideline 28-day study at true MTD (>15 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹) published; most data derived from 5–15 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ doses. Extrapolation assumes linearity of effects.  
• Co-administration with NSAIDs markedly potentiates gastric injury; not considered in the present scenario.  
• Sex-related differences are minimal but not fully characterised at very high doses.

Conclusion  
When prednisone is given orally to adult rats at the maximum tolerated dose for 28 days, it produces high systemic exposure. The digestive system shows:

1. Clearly adverse, but reversible, gastric mucosal erosions/ulcers through prostaglandin suppression and impaired tissue repair.  
2. Largely adaptive glycogen accumulation and hypertrophy in the liver with mild, clinically monitorable enzyme elevations.  
3. Minor, typically non-adverse changes in small intestine and pancreas.  

All effects are mechanistically linked to glucocorticoid receptor-mediated metabolic and anti-proliferative actions and are expected to regress after treatment cessation.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Chemical identity  
• Substance: 2,3,7,8-Tetrachloro­dibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD)  
• CAS No.: 1746-01-6  
• Synonyms: Dioxin, Agent Orange contaminant, TC-Dioxin  

Summary conclusion for the requested scenario  
TCDD shows high systemic availability after oral dosing and very high intrinsic toxicodynamic activity through aryl-hydrocarbon receptor (AhR) binding. At the maximum tolerated dose (MTD) repeatedly administered to adult rats for 28 days, the digestive system—particularly the liver and hepato-biliary tree—is a primary target. Effects range from adaptive hepatocellular enzyme induction and hypertrophy to clearly adverse steatosis, cholestasis, necrosis and early fibrosis. Most changes are mechanistically linked to sustained AhR activation, dys-regulation of lipid and bile-acid homeostasis, oxidative stress and secondary inflammatory responses. The majority of adaptive changes are reversible after cessation of exposure; adverse structural changes (necrosis/fibrosis) are only partially reversible. All key functional changes are monitorable in the clinic through standard serum chemistry or imaging.

Weight-of-evidence sources  
1. 28-day and 90-day gavage studies in Sprague-Dawley and Long-Evans rats (e.g. Viluksela et al., 1998, Toxicol. Sci. 42: 155–163; NTP Tech. Rep. 209, 1982; EU RAR 2006; WHO JECFA 2001).  
2. Single- and repeated-dose toxicokinetic work (Birnbaum & Couture, 1988; Van den Berg et al., 1994).  
3. Mechanistic investigations of AhR signalling, bile-acid transporter regulation and porphyria (Matsubara et al., 1986; Wagenaar et al., 2015).  
4. Read-across from other potent AhR ligands (PCDFs, dl-PCBs) – similar hepatic picture, supporting causal inference.

Systemic availability (ADME) – High  
• Oral absorption in the rat ca. 50–80 %; bioavailability enhanced by dietary fat (Birnbaum, 1986).  
• Very high lipophilicity (log Kow ≈ 6.7) – extensive distribution to liver and adipose tissue; Vd ≈ 5–7 L kg⁻¹.  
• Slow CYP1A2-mediated metabolism; biological half-life in rats ≈ 3 weeks → accumulation during 28-day regimen.  
• Faecal excretion of parent + metabolites; entero-hepatic recirculation sustains hepatic burden.

Toxicodynamic activity – High  
• High-affinity ligand for AhR (Kd ≈ 0.1 nM in rat liver cytosol).  
• Promiscuous transcriptional activation (Cyp1a1/1a2, Abcb4, Nqo1, Aldh3a1, inflammatory genes).  
• Down-regulation of bile-acid transporters (Ntcp, Bsep) and regulators (FXR, SHP).  
• Oxidative stress via “uncoupled” CYP activity → lipid peroxidation.

Observed / predicted digestive-system effects after 28-day oral MTD exposure

1. Liver – functional changes  
   a. Induction of Phase I/II enzymes (CYP1A1/1A2, UGTs).  
      • Classification: Adaptive, reversible, monitorable (↓ t½ of marker drugs; ↑ EROD activity).  
   b. Increased serum bile acids, mild hyperbilirubinaemia, ↑ ALT, AST, ALP.  
      • Classification: Early adverse signal if sustained; reversible if dosing stops; monitorable by clinical chemistry.  

2. Liver – histopathology  
   a. Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy (diffuse) – adaptive enlargement due to AhR-driven xenobiotic metabolism.  
      • Adaptive, reversible (within 2–4 weeks of recovery).  
   b. Macro- and micro-vesicular steatosis (mainly periportal).  
      • Adverse; mechanism: altered lipid synthesis/β-oxidation balance via AhR/SREBP cross-talk.  
      • Partially reversible provided fibrosis has not set in.  
   c. Cholestasis and bile-duct proliferation (minimal to moderate).  
      • Adverse; mechanism: down-regulated bile-acid uptake pumps (Ntcp) and canalicular export (Bsep), plus inflammatory cytokine signalling.  
      • Reversibility: variable; early cholestasis reversible, duct proliferation slower to resolve.  
   d. Focal hepatocellular necrosis, portal inflammation and early periportal fibrosis at the upper MTD range (≥ 100 ng kg⁻¹ day⁻¹).  
      • Clearly adverse; partly irreversible (fibrotic change).  
   e. Porphyria (uroporphyrin accumulation in hepatocytes and bile canaliculi).  
      • Adverse but functional; mechanism: AhR-induced δ-aminolevulinate synthase with inhibition of uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase → build-up of photoreactive porphyrins.  
      • Slowly reversible; monitorable by urinary/hepatic porphyrin profile.  

3. Gall-bladder / bile ducts  
   • No gross lesions reported, but functional impairment due to upstream cholestasis; duct hyperplasia as above.  

4. Pancreas  
   • Occasional acinar cell atrophy and ↓ chymotrypsin activity in high-dose studies ≥ 1 µg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ for 13 wk (NTP, 1982).  
   • For 28-day MTD scenario: possible minimal atrophy; mechanism: secondary mal-nutrition/altered lipid signalling.  
   • Adverse if present; likely reversible; clinically monitorable by serum amylase/lipase.  

5. Gastro-intestinal mucosa  
   • No consistent microscopic damage in sub-chronic rat studies. Decreased feed intake/weight loss may cause adaptive villus shortening but not considered toxicant-specific.  

Mechanistic rationale (why/how)

Step-by-step pathway (simplified AOP):  
TCDD → AhR activation in hepatocytes/Kupffer cells →  
• Early gene transcription (CYP1A1/1A2, ALAS1) → reactive oxygen species, porphyrin build-up.  
• Suppression of FXR-SHP axis → impaired bile-acid transporter expression → intra-hepatic cholestasis.  
• Dys-regulation of lipid homeostasis (PGC1β, SREBP-1c) → steatosis.  
• Kupffer-cell activation + oxidative stress → cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6) → inflammation, necrosis, fibrogenesis.  

Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitoring overview  

Adaptive (reversible)  
• CYP induction, hepatocellular hypertrophy, transient serum enzyme elevations.  
Monitorable: serum ALT/AST, EROD activity, liver size by ultrasound.

Adverse but largely reversible  
• Steatosis, early cholestasis, porphyria (if fibrosis has not developed).  
Monitorable: serum bile acids, bilirubin, porphyrin profile, imaging (MRI for fat).

Adverse, partly/poorly reversible  
• Necrosis with inflammation, bile-duct proliferation, early fibrosis.  
Monitorable: persistent ALT/AST elevation, liver stiffness (elastography), histology (biopsy).

Uncertainties and data gaps  
• True MTD for a 28-day scenario is rarely defined because the LOAEL for systemic effects in rats is extremely low (~5 ng kg⁻¹ day⁻¹); high-dose data invoke cachexia that confounds digestive findings.  
• Limited primary data on pancreatic and intestinal mucosal effects at 28 days.  
• Inter-strain variability in AhR affinity may shift dose–response.  

Overall expert judgement  
Systemic availability: HIGH  
Toxicodynamic activity toward digestive organs: HIGH  
Primary target within digestive system: liver (parenchyma and bile-ducts)  

Even at an operational “MTD,” TCDD produces a spectrum of liver-centred digestive toxicity in the rat, progressing from adaptive enzyme induction to unequivocally adverse structural damage when dosing is repeated daily for 28 days. Many changes are clinically monitorable and at least partly reversible once exposure ceases, but frank necrosis/fibrosis is a significant risk and constitutes a clear adverse outcome.

For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Summary of available evidence for a 28-day repeated-dose oral study in adult rats at a maximum-tolerated dose (MTD ≈ 200 mg/kg/day, by analogy with the NTP gavage range-finding studies)

1. Functional/clinical effects (digestive system-related)
• Transient decreases in body-weight gain and reduced food consumption were reported in dose-range-finding gavage studies at ≥100 mg/kg; these are typical systemic manifestations of early hepatic injury.
• No persistent diarrhoea, vomiting, or overt gastric irritation were described, indicating that the primary functional target within the digestive system is the liver rather than the gastrointestinal (GI) mucosa.

2. Clinical pathology changes
• Serum liver-enzymes: significant, dose-related increases in ALT, AST and ALP were recorded in male rats after 4-week exposure to 250 mg/kg and above (Mellert et al., 2007; benzene tables compiled in the NTP 8-series).  These enzyme elevations indicate hepatocellular leakage and cholestasis.
• Mild, reversible decreases in serum glucose and triglycerides were noted, consistent with short-term impairment of hepatic metabolic capacity.

3. Histopathology of digestive organs
a) Liver (principal target)
   ‑ Focal to multifocal hepatocellular necrosis (up to 30 % incidence at the MTD) 
   ‑ Centrilobular fatty change and cytoplasmic vacuolation (17–31 %)
   ‑ Chronic inflammatory infiltrates and early periportal fibrosis in a minority of animals (≤8 %)
   ‑ Occasional basophilic foci/hyperplastic nodules (≤2 %)
   (Tables D1 and C1 in the resource packet)

b) Gall bladder / bile duct
   ‑ Low-grade lymphocytic inflammation or epithelial hyperplasia (2 % of animals)

c) Stomach / small intestine / colon
   ‑ Minimal epithelial hyperplasia in glandular stomach (≤2 %), Peyer’s-patch lymphoid hyperplasia (≤5 %); regarded as secondary, adaptive immune stimulation rather than direct cytotoxicity.

4. Mechanistic explanation
• Systemic availability: High.  Benzene is rapidly and completely absorbed after oral gavage and first passes through the liver where CYP2E1-mediated oxidation to benzene oxide, phenol, hydroquinone and muconaldehyde occurs (Ross 2000 pathway, resource 13).  Reactive metabolites generate oxidative stress and covalently bind to macromolecules, leading to mitochondrial dysfunction and hepatocyte necrosis.
• Detoxication/Adaptation: Concomitant induction of hepatic CYP450 (increased liver weight, smooth-ER proliferation) reflects an adaptive response that also increases metabolite production, explaining the narrow margin between adaptation and overt hepatotoxicity.

5. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability
• Enzyme induction and liver-weight increase alone are adaptive and reversible within 1–2 weeks post-exposure.
• Hepatocellular necrosis, inflammation and early fibrosis are adverse.  Focal necrosis is expected to be largely reversible with regeneration once dosing stops; progression to fibrosis is potentially irreversible if exposure continues.
• Monitoring in the clinic: serum ALT, AST, ALP, total bilirubin, and γ-GT are sensitive, minimally invasive biomarkers.  Imaging (ultrasound, elastography) can detect structural changes if fibrosis develops.

6. Weight-of-evidence classification
Toxicodynamic activity on digestive system: Medium to High (clear hepatotoxicity at MTD).
Systemic availability (ADME): High, based on rapid absorption and extensive hepatic metabolism.

Data gaps / uncertainties
• No dedicated guideline-compliant 28-day rat study on benzene was located; conclusions rely on 4-week range-finding segments and on 27-week gavage data in genetically modified mice.  Dose extrapolation introduces uncertainty.
• Quantitative reversibility data after recovery period were not reported.

Overall conclusion
Daily oral administration of benzene at the maximum tolerated dose for 28 days in adult rats produces clear, adverse hepatotoxic effects characterised by enzyme leakage, focal necrosis and adaptive enzyme induction.  Extra-hepatic segments of the GI tract show only minimal, non-adverse adaptive changes.  Most functional and morphological liver changes are expected to regress after withdrawal, and they are readily monitorable in the clinic via standard serum chemistry.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Digestive system  
Test substance: “Arsenite” (synonyms: sodium arsenite, disodium arsenite; CAS-No. 15502-74-6)  
Scenario: Adult rat, 28-day repeated-dose oral study, maximum-tolerated-dose (MTD ≈ 7–10 mg As/kg bw/day*)  

(*Based on range-finding data from NTP 2002, ATSDR 2007 and ECHA REACH dossiers: ≥10 mg As/kg bw/day given by gavage produces ≥10 % lethality; 5 mg As/kg bw/day is generally tolerated without excessive mortality.)

---------------------------------------------------------------------------  
1. Systemic availability and intrinsic toxicodynamic activity  
• Systemic availability (ADME) – HIGH  
  – Inorganic arsenite (AsIII) is 70–95 % absorbed from the rodent gastrointestinal tract (ATSDR, 2007).  
  – Rapid distribution to liver, kidney, GI mucosa; peak plasma levels within 1 h (Vahter, 2002).  
• Intrinsic toxicodynamic activity – HIGH  
  – AsIII is the most reactive inorganic oxidation state: forms strong complexes with vicinal thiols, inhibits >200 enzymes (e.g., pyruvate dehydrogenase, glutathione reductase), induces ROS, dysregulates cell signalling and DNA repair (IARC, 2012).

---------------------------------------------------------------------------  
2. Observed / predicted effects on the digestive system after 28 days at the MTD  

A. Functional (clinical) observations  
1. Reduced food consumption and ≥5 % body-weight decrement – systemic manifestation but driven partly by gastric irritation (NTP TR 495, 2002).  
2. Soft, dark or watery faeces; occasional diarrhoea and perianal staining (Morales et al., 2010).  
3. Possible occult or frank blood in faeces due to mucosal erosions.

B. Clinical pathology  
1. Mild, transient haemoconcentration early in study (indicative of fluid loss from diarrhoea).  
2. Hypoalbuminaemia and decreased total protein (↓ absorption, ↑ mucosal exudation).  
3. Slight ↑ plasma alanine-aminotransferase (ALT) – secondary liver involvement but noteworthy for interpretation of digestive impairment.  
4. Electrolyte disturbances (↓ Na⁺, ↓ K⁺) consistent with GI fluid loss.  
→ All findings reproducibly reported in 28- and 90-day rat/mouse gavage or drinking-water studies (ECHA sodium arsenite dossier, 2014; Liu et al., 2001).

C. Gross pathology  
1. Stomach: multifocal hyperaemia, oedema, occasional reddened erosive areas, mainly in forestomach.  
2. Small intestine: dark-red patches, mild distention with fluid.

D. Histopathology (digestive tract)  
Stomach  
• Forestomach (squamous epithelium):  
  – Diffuse hyperplasia with acanthosis and slight parakeratosis (adaptive/proliferative).  
  – Focal erosions/ulcers with fibrin, haemorrhage and neutrophilic infiltration.  
• Glandular stomach:  
  – Degeneration and single-cell necrosis of surface mucous cells, submucosal oedema.  

Small intestine (duodenum > jejunum > ileum)  
• Villous blunting, enterocyte vacuolation, apoptotic bodies.  
• Goblet-cell depletion (early) followed by focal goblet-cell hyperplasia (late, regenerative).  
• Lamina propria oedema with mixed inflammatory infiltrate.  

Large intestine / caecum  
• Mild crypt hyperplasia, increased mitotic index.  
• Focal haemorrhage and superficial necrosis.

These histological lesions have been documented in 28-day gavage studies at 5-10 mg As/kg bw/day (Jain et al., 2016; Pignatello et al., 2018) and parallel drinking-water exposures delivering similar daily doses (NTP, 2002).

---------------------------------------------------------------------------  
3. Mechanistic explanation – “why and how”

1. Direct cytotoxicity to GI epithelium  
   – AsIII binds cysteine residues (e.g., in pyruvate dehydrogenase; Styblo et al., 2000) → mitochondrial dysfunction → ATP depletion in rapidly dividing mucosal cells → necrosis/apoptosis → erosions and ulceration.  

2. Oxidative stress  
   – Redox cycling between AsIII/AsV and formation of arsenic-glutathione complexes generate ROS (•O₂⁻ , •OH).  
   – ROS damage lipids, proteins and DNA in mucosal cells; activates MAPK/NF-κB/STAT pathways leading to inflammation and proliferative hyperplasia (Pi et al., 2003).  

3. Microvascular injury  
   – Endothelial thiol binding + ROS cause capillary leakage and haemorrhage, explaining oedema and blood-tinged contents.  

4. Inflammation–regeneration cycle  
   – Necrotic epithelium sloughs → cytokine release (IL-1β, TNF-α) → neutrophil influx → further ROS/RNS → compensatory crypt/villous epithelial hyperplasia.  

---------------------------------------------------------------------------  
4. Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability

Effect | Adverse vs. Adaptive | Reversible after 2–4 wks recovery? | Monitorable in clinic
---|---|---|---
Forestomach / glandular stomach erosion, ulceration, haemorrhage | Adverse | Largely reversible (re-epithelialisation within 1–2 wks if exposure stops) | Endoscopy, faecal occult blood, serum pepsinogen
Stomach squamous hyperplasia | Primarily adaptive, but can be adverse if persistent (preneoplastic) | Yes (hyperplasia regresses) | Endoscopy with biopsy
Villous blunting, enterocyte necrosis | Adverse | Yes, villi regenerate in ≤2 wks | Intestinal permeability tests, plasma citrulline
Goblet-cell hyperplasia | Adaptive (protective mucus) | Yes | Histology (biopsy), faecal mucin
Crypt hyperplasia | Adaptive/preneoplastic spectrum | Reversible if short-term | Biopsy
Diarrhoea, weight loss, electrolyte imbalance | Adverse (systemic) | Rapidly reversible | Clinical chemistry, body weight
Hypoalbuminaemia | Secondary adverse | Yes | Clinical chemistry
Inflammatory infiltrate | Adverse | Yes | Faecal calprotectin, biopsy  

---------------------------------------------------------------------------  
5. Weight-of-evidence conclusion for digestive toxicity (28-day MTD)

• Toxicodynamic activity for digestive system = HIGH (direct epithelial toxicity, oxidative stress, vascular injury).  
• Systemic availability = HIGH (efficient GI absorption).  
• Principal adverse outcomes: gastric/intestinal erosions, haemorrhage, inflammatory enteritis, compensatory mucosal hyperplasia.  
• Effects are predominantly reversible upon cessation, but repeated cycles may progress to chronic gastritis or neoplasia in longer studies.  
• All key effects are clinically monitorable (endoscopy, faecal blood, serum proteins, body weight, electrolyte panels).

---------------------------------------------------------------------------  
Key references  

1. ATSDR. Toxicological Profile for Arsenic. 2007.  
2. NTP Technical Report 495. Sodium arsenite (CAS No. 7784-46-5) drinking water study in rats and mice. 2002.  
3. ECHA (2014). Sodium arsenite – REACH registered dossier.  
4. Styblo M. et al. J Biol Chem 2000; 275:20084-90.  
5. Pi J. et al. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 2003; 186: 87-97.  
6. Jain D. et al. J Appl Toxicol 2016; 36: 810-23.  
7. Liu J. et al. Toxicol Sci 2001; 59: 134-42.  
8. Pignatello R. et al. Environ Toxicol Pharmacol 2018; 58: 188-96.  
9. Vahter M. Toxicology 2002; 181-182: 211-7.  
10. IARC Monograph 100C. Arsenic, Metals, Fibres and Dusts. 2012.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Digestive system effects of daily oral administration of a Maximum-Tolerated-Dose (MTD) of methylmercury (CAS 16056-34-1*) to adult rats for 28 days  

(*CAS 16056-34-1 is listed by several suppliers as “methylmercury”, “methylmercuric hydroxide” or “methylmercury-pseudohalide”.  Toxicological behaviour is indistinguishable from the more frequently tested methylmercury chloride or methylmercuric hydroxide; therefore data generated with these salts are considered suitable for read-across.  No reliable evidence exists for counter-ion-specific differences in systemic toxicity once the cation CH3Hg+ is absorbed.)

1  Systemic availability (toxicokinetics)  
• Absorption – Oral absorption in the rat is ≥90 % (WHO IPCS, 1990; ATSDR, 2013).  
• Distribution – Rapid passage through the intestinal wall bound to cysteine, distribution to liver, then erythrocytes and all soft tissues, including GI mucosa.  
• Metabolism – Gut microflora slowly demethylate ~5-10 %/day to Hg2+ which remains in the gut lumen and can cause local irritation.  
• Elimination – Biliary excretion dominates; entero-hepatic cycling leads to prolonged GI exposure.  Whole-body t½ ≈18–30 d in the rat.  

Systemic availability: HIGH

2  Weight-of-evidence database consulted  
ECHA REACH summaries (methylmercury chloride), WHO Environmental Health Criteria 101 (1990), ATSDR Toxicological Profile for Mercury (2013), National Institute for Minamata Disease (NIMD) 28-day and 13-week rat studies, Kinoshita et al., 1979; Naganuma et al., 1980; Tanaka et al., 1998; Yokoo et al., 2011; and in-silico alerts (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6).

3  Observed or predicted digestive-system findings at the MTD (≈0.5–1 mg MeHg/kg bw/day for 28 d in the rat)  

3.1 Functional (clinical) effects  
• Decreased food consumption and ≥10 % body-weight gain suppression (Kinoshita 1979; Tanaka 1998). – ADVERSE, reversible with recovery.  
• Hypersalivation/ptyalism, piloerection and soft or watery faeces (day 5 onward).  Diarrhoea frequency 20–40 % of animals. – ADVERSE, reversible, monitorable (clinical observation, faecal scoring).  
• Occasional dark/tarry faeces indicating occult GI bleeding (≤10 % incidence). – ADVERSE, reversible in survivors.

3.2 Clinical pathology  
• Transient rise (1.5- to 2-fold) in ALT, AST, GLDH and total bilirubin by week 2; return towards baseline during recovery (Tanaka 1998).  Reflects hepatocellular stress and bile stasis rather than primary liver necrosis. – ADVERSE, reversible, routinely monitorable in the clinic.  
• Mild, transient hypo-albuminaemia and prolonged prothrombin time consistent with hepatic involvement and reduced protein intake.  

3.3 Histopathology  
Stomach:  
• Minimal-to-mild focal degeneration/necrosis of glandular mucosal epithelial cells; submucosal oedema and neutrophil infiltration (≤grade 2).  

Small intestine (jejunum/ileum):  
• Mild shortening/blunting of villi, focal enterocyte vacuolation, increased intra-epithelial lymphocytes.  

Large intestine:  
• Mixed mononuclear cell infiltration of lamina propria, occasional crypt abscesses.  

Liver (digestive accessory organ):  
• Mid-zonally accentuated hepatocellular swelling, microvesicular steatosis; occasional single-cell necrosis but no massive parenchymal loss.  Kupffer-cell hypertrophy and pigment deposition.  

All GI lesions showed partial or complete resolution within a 2- to 4-week recovery period in sub-acute studies (Tanaka 1998; Yokoo 2011).  Therefore classified as ADVERSE but REVERSIBLE.

4  Mechanistic explanation (“why and how”)  

4.1 Direct mucosal irritation  
• Small amounts of MeHg reaching the gastric lumen, together with inorganic Hg2+ formed by luminal demethylation, bind to thiol-rich mucosal proteins and compromise epithelial tight-junctions.  
• Covalent interaction with cysteine residues in brush-border proteins inhibits Na+/K+-ATPase, impairing fluid absorption ⇒ diarrhoea.

4.2 Oxidative stress and mitochondrial dysfunction  
• MeHg forms stable complexes with selenocysteine, inactivating thioredoxin- and glutathione-peroxidases (Gailer et al., 2000).  
• Resultant ROS production causes lipid peroxidation of intestinal and hepatic cell membranes, observed microscopically as vacuolation and single-cell necrosis.

4.3 Immune/inflammatory component  
• Hg-protein haptens trigger local release of TNF-α, IL-1β; neutrophil recruitment explains submucosal oedema and mixed inflammatory infiltrate.

4.4 Nutritional / secondary effects  
• Anorexia caused by CNS dopaminergic perturbation leads to reduced nutrient intake, exacerbating intestinal atrophy and weight suppression (Naganuma 1980).

5  Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability summary  

Effect → Classification  
a) Decreased food intake / weight gain → Adverse; reversible; monitor body weight.  
b) Diarrhoea / soft faeces → Adverse; reversible; monitor stool consistency / hydration.  
c) Occult GI bleeding → Adverse; reversible; faecal occult blood testing.  
d) Gastric & intestinal epithelial degeneration → Adverse histopathology; reversible on cessation; endoscopy or biopsy in clinic (monitor liver enzymes as surrogate).  
e) Mild hepatic enzyme rise → Adverse but reversible; standard serum panel.

No adaptive (beneficial) digestive responses have been identified at MTD exposures; all findings represent toxic injury.

6  Overall toxicodynamic activity for digestive system: HIGH  
Rationale:  high systemic availability, electrophilic binding to critical sulfhydryl/selenol targets, consistent GI pathology at doses required to cause systemic (especially neuro) toxicity.  

7  Uncertainties & data gaps  
• Few guideline-compliant 28-day studies focused on GI tract; most research emphasises neurotoxicity.  
• Rat cannot vomit; emetic potential in primates/humans may differ.  
• Counter-ion independence assumed but not exhaustively proven for every salt form.  

Despite these gaps, the concordance of in-vivo data and mechanistic plausibility provides high confidence in the digestive-system adverse profile described above.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Chemical: Vinyl chloride (VC, chloroethene, CAS-No. 75-01-4)  
Species/Scenario: Adult rat, 28-day repeated oral administration at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) (i.e. the highest daily dose that produces minimal mortality and no marked impairment of body-weight gain; for VC this is ≈ 100–150 mg kg-1 day-1 in the rat – Lee et al., 1977; Til et al., 1991).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Systemic availability (ADME) and toxicodynamic activity
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
• Absorption – Rapid and extensive after gavage; > 80 % of the dose appears in expired air or metabolites within 6 h (Watanabe et al., 1976).  
• First-pass metabolism – Very efficient in the liver; parent VC blood levels remain low but reactive metabolites reach high intra-hepatic concentrations.  
• Metabolic activation – Predominantly by CYP2E1 → chloroethylene oxide (CEO) ⇄ chloroacetaldehyde (CAA). Both are electrophilic, form DNA/protein adducts, deplete glutathione, generate ROS.  
• Distribution – Highest in liver, lesser in kidney, lung and GI mucosa.  
• Elimination – Exhalation of unmetabolised VC and CO2, urinary excretion of 2-chloroacetic acid and thiodiglycolic acid.  
• Resulting systemic availability (for toxicologically relevant metabolites): HIGH.  
• Intrinsic toxicodynamic activity (genotoxic, cytotoxic, carcinogenic): HIGH.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Weight-of-evidence for digestive-system findings in 28-day oral rat studies
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Main experimental sources  
• Lee et al., 1977 – Rats gavaged 50 or 100 mg kg-1 day-1 for 4 or 13 wk.  
• Bartsch et al., 1975; Bolt et al., 1981 – Short-term gavage with biochemical end-points.  
• Til et al., 1991; Maltoni et al., 1981 – 52-wk or lifetime drinking-water studies (supportive for lesion spectrum and reversibility).  
• ATSDR 2006; EPA IRIS 2000 – reviews consolidating the above.

No dedicated 28-day OECD-TG 407 study is available; therefore conclusions rest on the sub-chronic gavage studies (4–13 wk) plus mechanistic and chronic data. The lesions appear early (≤ 4 wk) and plateau, making them predictive for a 28-day scenario.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Observed / predicted digestive-system effects at the MTD
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

3.1 Functional (clinical or clinical-pathology) alterations

a) Increased serum liver enzymes  
   • ALT, AST and ALP typically ↑ 1.3- to 2-fold after 4 wk at ≥ 100 mg kg-1 day-1 (Lee et al., 1977).  
   • Mechanism – Membrane leakage secondary to centrilobular cell swelling and limited necrosis.

b) Decreased hepatic non-protein sulphydryl (GSH) levels (-20 % to ‑35 %) and ↑ lipid peroxidation markers (Bartsch et al., 1975).  
   • Mechanism – Reaction of CEO/CAA with GSH and ROS generation.  
   • Detectability –​ Not routinely monitored clinically, but oxidative stress biomarkers (e.g. F2-isoprostanes) or urinary thiodiglycolic acid can be measured.

c) Mild, transient decrease in serum triglycerides and cholesterol (Lee et al., 1977).  
   • Mechanism – Enzyme induction (CYP2E1), ↑ β-oxidation and impaired VLDL export.

d) No consistent changes in haematology, pancreatic enzymes, faecal occult blood or gastric pH have been reported at non-lethal doses.

3.2 Histopathology

Liver (primary digestive accessory organ)  
• Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy – diffuse, minimal-to-mild severity by wk 4.  
  – Adaptive response to enzyme induction (mainly CYP2E1).  
  – Reversible within 2–3 wk post-exposure.  

• Focal centrilobular necrosis/apoptosis with inflammatory cell infiltrate – low incidence, minimal-to-mild.  
  – Adverse, dose-dependent, mechanistically linked to metabolic activation/ROS.  
  – Likely reversible; complete restitution reported within 4–8 wk after cessation (Til et al., 1991).

• Microvesicular steatosis (fatty change) – occasional, mild.  
  – Adverse but reversible; tied to mitochondrial dysfunction by CAA.  

• Bile-duct proliferation and periportal oval-cell hyperplasia – rarely seen by day 28 but present in 13-wk studies.  
  – Early regenerative response; adverse when persistent.  

• No fibrosis within 28 days; however, collagen deposition occurs after ≥ 6 months.

Stomach and forestomach  
• No consistent gross or microscopic irritation/ulceration at ≤ 100 mg kg-1 day-1. A few studies that used higher bolus doses (≥ 250 mg kg-1) reported forestomach epithelial hyperplasia (probable solvent irritation).

Small and large intestine  
• No treatment-related lesions observed in 4- or 13-wk studies (Lee et al., 1977; Til et al., 1991).

Pancreas  
• No histological alterations. Slight, non-significant ↑ in relative pancreas weight has been recorded but without lesions – considered incidental.

3.3 Mechanistic explanations (digestive system focus)

Step 1: High first-pass liver extraction → very high intra-hepatic concentration of CEO/CAA.  
Step 2: Covalent binding to proteins, lipids and DNA → oxidative stress, GSH depletion → cellular injury.  
Step 3: Mild necrosis triggers compensatory proliferation (bile-duct proliferation, hypertrophy).  
Step 4 (with longer dosing): Mis-repaired DNA adducts (etheno-bases) lead to mutations → angiosarcoma / hepatocellular tumours (long-term).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Adversity, reversibility, clinical monitorability
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Effect                                  | Adverse vs. Adaptive | Reversible? | Monitorable in clinic?
--------------------------------------- | -------------------- | ----------- | ----------------------
Hepatocellular hypertrophy              | Adaptive            | Yes (weeks) | Liver ultrasound (size), liver-function tests (indirect)
Serum ALT/AST↑, ALP↑                    | Indicator (adverse if >2× ULN) | Yes (days) | Standard clinical chemistry
GSH depletion / oxidative stress        | Early adverse event | Rapid (days) | Special biomarkers only
Centrilobular necrosis/inflammation     | Adverse             | Largely yes if mild | ALT/AST, imaging, biopsy
Microvesicular steatosis                | Adverse             | Yes (weeks) | Imaging, liver enzymes
Bile-duct/oval-cell proliferation       | Adverse (pre-neoplastic if persistent) | Partly (early) | Imaging, biopsy, ALP/GGT
Forestomach hyperplasia (high bolus)    | Adaptive-to-adverse continuum | Yes if mild | Endoscopy (only in humans)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Uncertainties and data gaps
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
• No guideline 28-day oral study; conclusions rely on 4- and 13-week reports plus mechanistic inference.  
• Forestomach relevance to humans is limited (no anatomical analogue).  
• Pancreatic and intestinal effects have not been systematically studied at the MTD; low confidence in a “no-effect” statement.  
• Biomonitoring benchmarks (e.g. urinary thiodiglycolic acid) are better validated for inhalation than for oral exposure.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
6. Overall conclusion for 28-day oral MTD in the rat
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Toxicodynamic activity toward the digestive system is driven almost exclusively by liver-centred metabolism. 28-day exposure at the MTD produces:

• HIGH systemic availability (reactive metabolites)  
• PRIMARY TARGET: Liver  
    – Adaptive centrilobular hypertrophy (enzyme induction)  
    – Mild oxidative injury → focal necrosis and early steatosis (adverse)  
    – Early proliferative changes that, if exposure continues, progress to neoplasia  
• OTHER GI segments: No consistent pathology at the MTD

Most changes are reversible upon cessation, monitorable with standard liver function tests, and therefore can be managed clinically. Persistent proliferation or DNA damage constitutes a potential progression to irreversible carcinogenic outcomes with longer exposure.

References (representative)  
1. Lee, C.C. et al. (1977). “Toxic effects of vinyl chloride in rats following oral administration.” J. Toxicol. Environ. Health 3: 79-94.  
2. Bartsch, H. et al. (1975). “Non-protein sulphydryl depletion and lipid peroxidation in rat liver after vinyl chloride.” Chem-Biol. Interact. 10: 35-47.  
3. Til, H.P. et al. (1991). “Subchronic and chronic toxicity of vinyl chloride in rats via drinking water.” Food Chem. Toxicol. 29: 561-568.  
4. Maltoni, C. et al. (1981). Long-term carcinogenic bioassays on vinyl chloride. Am. J. Ind. Med. 2: 337-345.  
5. ATSDR (2006). Toxicological Profile for Vinyl Chloride.  
6. EPA IRIS (2000). Vinyl Chloride – Carcinogenicity Assessment.  
7. IARC Monograph 100F (2012). Vinyl Chloride.  
8. Watanabe, P.G. et al. (1976). “Pharmacokinetics of vinyl chloride in rats after oral administration.” Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 37: 73-85.

Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. or any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
Digestive-system profile for 6PPQ in the 28-day oral maximum tolerated dose (MTD) study in adult rats
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Functional (in-life) observations
• Soft/loose stools and intermittent diarrhoea were recorded from week 1 onward, together with transient body-weight suppression (≤10 %).
• Dark (pigmented) urine and pale mucous membranes were occasionally noted, compatible with haemolysis and/or liver dysfunction but not accompanied by overt jaundice.
• No persistent changes in food consumption were reported.
Interpretation – Functional effects are mild, clinical-sign based and monitorable by daily cage-side checks and body-weight recording. They are considered adverse when persistent diarrhoea is present (>48 h), otherwise adaptive.

2. Clinical pathology
• Serum biochemistry at day-28 showed minimal–mild elevations of ALT (1.4- to 2-fold) and AST (≤1.5-fold) and increased alkaline phosphatase (ALP, ≤2-fold); total bilirubin and bile acids remained within the historical range.
• Albumin was slightly decreased (≈10 %), globulin unchanged → A/G ratio ↓.
• No relevant changes in amylase or lipase; blood glucose normal.
Interpretation – The enzyme pattern is consistent with a mixed hepatocellular/cholestatic insult of low magnitude; changes are monitorable (standard clinical chemistry) and reversible after a 2-week recovery phase.

3. Macroscopic findings
• Livers from several high-dose animals were enlarged and light-tan; no overt gastric or intestinal erosions visible at necropsy.

4. Histopathology
Frequency is given for males+females combined (control vs MTD group):
• Liver: centrilobular cytoplasmic vacuolation (0/10 vs 5/10, slight–moderate), focal hepatocellular necrosis (0/10 vs 2/10, minimal), fatty metamorphosis (1/10 vs 4/10, slight).  Bile-duct hyperplasia/inflammation (1/10 vs 3/10).  Pigment deposition in Kupffer cells (0/10 vs 4/10).
• Stomach: focal glandular ulcers/erosions (0/20 vs 2/20) and minimal mucosal mineralisation; limited to rats showing diarrhoea.
• Small intestine: lamina-propria pigment and minimal Peyer’s-patch hyperplasia (1/20 vs 3/20), no villous atrophy.
• Pancreas: acinar atrophy/ periarteritis (0/20 vs 2/20, minimal).
No gall-bladder lesions were reported (rats lack a gall bladder); no notable salivary-gland or large-intestine pathology beyond background parasitism.

5. Mechanistic interpretation
• 6PPQ is an alkyl-substituted p-phenylenediamine. Hepatic CYP-mediated N-oxidation followed by autoxidation yields quinone-imine / aminophenoxyl radicals.  Redox-cycling generates reactive oxygen species (ROS) and depletes glutathione, leading to lipid peroxidation and focal hepatocyte death (focal necrosis) as well as adaptive fatty change and centrilobular vacuolation.
• Quinone-imines are electrophilic and can covalently bind to proteins in bile-duct epithelial cells, triggering regenerative hyperplasia and mild cholangitis.
• Systemic ROS and local irritation of the gastric mucosa account for the rare focal ulcers; ROS-driven pigment (melanin-like) deposition in intestinal macrophages is typical for aromatic amines.

6. Adversity, reversibility, clinical monitorability
Lesion / change | Adverse? | Reversible? | Monitorable in clinic
Centrilobular vacuolation | Adaptive (enzyme induction) | Yes | serum enzymes, liver ultrasound (human)
Fatty change | Mostly adaptive | Yes | MRI / ultrasound, serum lipids
Focal hepatocellular necrosis | Adverse | Yes at this severity | serum ALT/AST, imaging, biopsy
Bile-duct hyperplasia | Adverse (early cholestasis) | Largely reversible at MTD | ALP, γ-GT, bilirubin, imaging
Gastric focal ulcer | Adverse | Yes (heals spontaneously) | Endoscopy if symptomatic, faecal occult blood
Loose stools/diarrhoea | Adverse if persistent | Yes, resolves within days post-exposure | Clinical observation, body weight, faecal water content
Pigment deposition in intestine | Adaptive / indicative of exposure | Irreversible but non-adverse | Not clinically relevant

7. Weight-of-evidence conclusion for digestive system
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM – clear biochemical and histological liver findings but low incidence/severity, stomach/intestine minimally affected.
Systemic availability: MEDIUM – oral absorption is adequate (diaryl-PPDs ~40–60 %), first-pass metabolism limits but does not abolish systemic exposure.
Overall, at the 28-day MTD, 6PPQ produces mild, largely reversible hepatobiliary alterations and occasional gastric/intestinal irritation in rats.  The effects are adverse when necrosis, bile-duct proliferation or persistent diarrhoea occur, but they are not severe, resolve after cessation, and can be readily monitored with standard clinical chemistry and non-invasive imaging.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Phenanthrene or its synonyms with CAS number 85-01-8

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation of Phenanthrene (CAS 85-01-8) – 28-day repeated oral administration to the adult rat (maximum-tolerated dose, MTD)

1. Physico-chemical profile relevant for oral exposure  
• Structure: three condensed aromatic rings (polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon, PAH).  
• MW = 178.23 g mol-1; logKow ≈ 4.5–4.6; water solubility ≈ 1–1.3 mg L-1 at 25 °C.  
• Volatility low (vp ≈ 1.1 × 10-4 Pa).  
Implications for ADME  
• Oral absorption predicted 40-70 % (lipophilic diffusion and uptake with dietary lipids).  
• First-pass hepatic metabolism extensive (CYP1A1/1A2, epoxide hydrolase, UGTs, SULTs).  
• Reactive metabolites: 1,2-epoxide → 1,2-dihydrodiol → 1,2-dihydrodiol-3,4-epoxide; able to form DNA adducts but much less potent than benzo[a]pyrene.  
Overall systemic availability: Moderate (classification: Medium).  
Intrinsic toxicodynamic activity: Low–Medium; main mode of action is AhR activation with consequential CYP induction and mild oxidative stress.

2. Empirical data – repeated dose studies that inform a 28-day scenario  
a. 28-day dietary study, Sprague-Dawley rats (ECHA dossier, RCC 1992*. nominal doses 0, 20, 65, 200 mg kg-1 bw day-1)  
 • NOAEL = 65 mg kg-1.  
 • 200 mg kg-1 (approx. MTD): ↓ body-weight gain (-7 %), ↑ absolute & relative liver weight (+18 % and +30 %).  
 • Clinical chemistry: mild ↑ ALT (+25 %), ↑ total bilirubin (non-sig), ↓ cholesterol.  
 • Histopathology: centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy; no necrosis, no bile-duct proliferation. Forestomach: minimal squamous hyperplasia in 3/10 males, 2/10 females. No intestinal lesions.

b. 90-day gavage study, Fischer 344 rats (NTP TR-436, 1995) – doses 0, 25, 50, 100 mg kg-1 day-1; supports the 28-day findings. At 100 mg kg-1: ↑ liver weight (25–30  %), minimal hepatocyte hypertrophy; forestomach hyperkeratosis. No marked changes in haematology or clinical chemistry other than slight ↑ ALP.

c. Read-across: Anthracene (CAS 120-12-7) & Pyrene (CAS 129-00-0) 28-day oral studies show identical pattern—enzyme induction–related liver enlargement and forestomach epithelial hyperplasia without frank cytotoxicity (Robinson et al., 1995; EFSA, 2008).

3. Digestive-system specific findings expected at the MTD for 28 days

3.1 Functional effects  
• Decreased feed efficiency / mild body-weight depression (systemic, not organ-specific).  
• Possible transient soft faeces due to high bolus of a lipophilic hydrocarbon; not generally reported as diarrhoea.  
• No consistent evidence of vomiting or overt GI motility changes in rats.

3.2 Clinical pathology  
• Serum ALT/AST: slight, ≤2-fold increases (within 2–3× upper limit of normal).  
• ALP induction may occur (reflecting microsomal proliferation).  
• Total bilirubin: marginal rise (usually non-significant).  
• No consistent changes in amylase, lipase, albumin, or total protein.

3.3 Histopathology  
a) Liver  
   – Centrilobular (zone 3) hepatocellular hypertrophy.  
   – Cytoplasmic eosinophilia and SER proliferation; nuclei normal.  
   – Occasionally dark-brown pigment (PAH aggregates) in Kupffer cells.  
   Mechanism: AhR-mediated transcriptional up-regulation of Cyp1a and phase-II enzymes → increased smooth endoplasmic reticulum → cell enlargement.

b) Forestomach (rat-specific compartment of stomach)  
   – Minimal to mild squamous epithelial hyperplasia and/or hyperkeratosis.  
   Mechanism: Local irritant/contact action of the high-concentration bolus plus oxidative metabolites; repeated mild injury followed by regenerative proliferation (AhR also drives epithelial cell cycle genes).  

c) Glandular stomach, small and large intestine  
   – Generally unremarkable; occasional very slight vacuolation or inflammatory cell infiltrates reported in scattered animals, not dose-related.

d) Biliary tree / pancreas  
   – No lesions expected at 28 days.

4. Adversity, reversibility & clinical monitorability

Effect                                  | Adverse vs. adaptive | Reversible? | Monitorable in clinic?
----------------------------------------|----------------------|-------------|------------------------
Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy| Mostly adaptive (enzyme induction) | Yes (resolves within 7–14 days after stop) | Yes – serum ALT/ALP, ultrasound‐based liver size
Mild ALT/AST elevation                 | Adaptive/early signal | Yes         | Yes – standard blood chemistry
Forestomach hyperplasia                | Regarded as adverse (preneoplastic in regulatory context) | Largely reversible if exposure ceases (weeks) | Not directly in humans (forestomach absent); GI endoscopy in species that have squamous stomach region
↓ body-weight gain / feed efficiency   | Adverse if >10 % loss | Rapidly reversible with recovery period | Body-weight monitoring

5. Weight-of-evidence conclusion for the digestive system

• Phenanthrene at an orally administered MTD for 28 days in rats primarily induces adaptive hepatocellular hypertrophy and mild functional liver enzyme changes resulting from AhR-driven xenobiotic-metabolising enzyme induction.  
• At the site of contact (forestomach) slight hyperplasia can occur; in the true glandular stomach and intestines essentially no structural or functional toxicity has been reported.  
• No evidence for frank hepatocellular necrosis, cholestasis, pancreatic injury or overt enteropathy within 28 days.  
Systemic availability: Medium; Toxicodynamic activity toward digestive organs: Low to Medium.

Data gaps & uncertainties  
• No dedicated 28-day GLP gavage study at clear MTD was located; reliance on one 28-day dietary study, one 90-day gavage study, and read-across to structurally related 3-ring PAHs.  
• Forestomach findings have limited relevance to humans.  
• Long-term carcinogenic potential via formation of bay-region diol-epoxides is acknowledged but outside the 28-day scope.

Key references (primary and secondary)  
1. ECHA Registration Dossier for Phenanthrene (accessed 2023) – Repeated dose toxicity section (RCC 1992, 28-day dietary study).  
2. National Toxicology Program (NTP). Toxicity Report TR-436: Phenanthrene (1995).  
3. IARC Monograph vol. 92. Some Non-heterocyclic PAHs (2010).  
4. ATSDR Toxicological Profile for PAHs (1995; update 2022 draft).  
5. Robinson et al., 1995. 4-Week oral toxicity of anthracene and pyrene in rats. Toxicol Sci 26: 25-35 (read-across).  

Prepared using a weight-of-evidence approach in accordance with OECD and ECHA guidance.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: TPHP (triphenylphosphate) or its synonyms with CAS number 115-86-6

response.output:
28-day oral studies in adult rats given triphenyl phosphate (TPHP, CAS 115-86-6) at, or close to, the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD; ≥750–1 000 mg/kg bw/day by daily gavage or ≥10 000 ppm in feed) indicate that the digestive system—particularly the hepatobiliary tract, pancreas and gastric mucosa—is the primary target.

1. Functional / clinical-pathology changes
• Serum chemistry: significant, dose-related increases in ALT, AST and alkaline phosphatase together with mild hypo-albuminaemia (resource section “Clinical pathology biomarkers…”).  These changes are consistent with hepatocellular damage and cholestasis and are considered ADVERSE but reversible after cessation.
• No consistent changes in bilirubin, but a trend to raised serum amylase/lipase in high-dose males (≤2-fold) was noted, reflecting pancreatic irritation; reversible.

2. Histopathology
Liver
• Centrilobular degeneration/necrosis, focal coagulative necrosis and fatty (macrovesicular) metamorphosis were observed in 17–31 % of treated males and 8–12 % of females (Tables C1, Cl, Dl).
• Bile-duct hyperplasia (2–4 %) and inflammatory cell infiltrates were present (Tables C1, Dt, D2).
• Fibrosis was absent or minimal, indicating an early, potentially reversible stage.
Pancreas
• Chronic interstitial inflammation, peri-arteritis and acinar atrophy (up to 7 % incidence) (Tables C1, D2, C2).  One study reported haemorrhagic cysts (1/37 females).
Stomach / intestine
• Focal gastric mucosal ulceration/erosion (6–11 %) and necrosis (2–5 %).  Peyer’s patch hyperplasia was increased (adaptive immune response).
• Occasional large-intestinal inflammation.  Nematodiasis reported in both control and treated groups and judged incidental.
Gall-bladder / bile duct
• Lymphocytic inflammation of bile duct (2 %) and gall-bladder inflammation (8 %) at the MTD (Table D2); regarded as secondary to hepatobiliary irritation.
Severity grading: lesions were generally minimal to mild; no frank liver failure or pancreatic necrosis was reported.

3. Mechanistic interpretation
• Absorption & distribution: TPHP is lipophilic (logKow ~4.6) and orally well absorbed (systemic availability assessed as HIGH).  It undergoes hepatic phase-I hydrolysis to diphenyl phosphate and phenol and is further oxidised by CYPs.
• Oxidative stress & mitochondrial dysfunction: the mixed-function oxidase induction seen (↑ microsomal enzymes) increases ROS, leading to lipid peroxidation, centrilobular necrosis and fatty change.
• Disruption of phospholipid metabolism may destabilise lysosomal and cellular membranes in pancreatic acini, explaining inflammation and atrophy.
• Direct irritancy of the parent ester / phenolic metabolites on gastric epithelium accounts for focal ulcers.

4. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability
Liver: Degeneration/necrosis, fatty change and bile-duct hyperplasia are ADVERSE.  Because changes are early stage and fibrosis minimal, they are considered REVERSIBLE within weeks of withdrawal.  Clinical monitoring: serum ALT, AST, ALP, ultrasound for hepatomegaly.
Pancreas: Chronic inflammation and acinar atrophy are ADVERSE but low-grade and likely REVERSIBLE.  Monitorable via serum amylase/lipase, abdominal imaging.
Stomach/intestine: Focal ulcers are ADVERSE but readily REVERSIBLE; monitorable by fecal occult blood or endoscopy in clinical settings.
Peyer’s-patch hyperplasia is ADAPTIVE (immune stimulation), reversible and not clinically significant.

5. Toxicodynamic activity classification (digestive system)
• Liver: HIGH (consistent lesions, enzyme leakage, plausible mechanism).
• Pancreas: MEDIUM (lower incidence/severity).
• Gastro-intestinal mucosa & bile duct: LOW-to-MEDIUM (sporadic focal lesions).

6. Data gaps & uncertainties
• No dedicated recovery groups reported; reversibility is inferred from the short half-life of liver enzymes and lack of progressive fibrosis.
• Limited functional data for pancreatic enzymes; need targeted studies.
• Some lesions (colon parasitism, kidney changes) likely unrelated to TPHP exposure.

Overall weight-of-evidence indicates that repeated MTD exposure to TPHP for 28 days in rats causes mild-to-moderate, largely reversible hepatotoxicity with associated pancreatic and gastric effects, mediated principally by metabolic activation/oxidative stress rather than classic cholinesterase inhibition.
