vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Lanthanum or its synonyms with CAS number 7439-91-0

response.output:
Toxicological profile of Lanthanum (metallic lanthanum; CAS 7439-91-0)
Species/sex/strain assumed in the question: adult rat (commonly Sprague-Dawley or Wistar)
Study design requested: oral, once-daily administration for 28 days at the Maximum-Tolerated-Dose (MTD).  
Main target organ requested: digestive system

1. SYSTEMIC AVAILABILITY (ADME) – Weight-of-evidence

• Oral absorption of elemental lanthanum or lanthanum salts in the rat is < 0.05 % of dose (Davies et al., 2004; EMA CHMP assessment report of Lanthanum carbonate, 2006).  
• Absorbed La is rapidly cleared by liver/bile and accumulates only slowly in bone and liver after repeated high dosing.  
• Therefore systemic availability is classified as LOW.  The effects described below result almost entirely from direct, luminal contact with the gastrointestinal (GI) mucosa.

2. OBSERVED / PREDICTED DIGESTIVE TRACT FINDINGS AFTER 28-DAY MTD DOSING

All findings below have been reported in 28- and 90-day rat studies with lanthanum metal powder, lanthanum chloride or, more extensively, lanthanum carbonate at dose levels that define the MTD (600–2 000 mg La/kg bw/day).  Because the toxicodynamic driver is the trivalent lanthanum cation (La3+) the effects are considered read-across applicable to elemental lanthanum.

2.1 Functional (clinical) observations
• Soft or liquid, often dark-grey/black faeces – due to unabsorbed La-phosphate complex; non-adverse, monitorable.  
• Transient decrease in feed intake and slight body-weight retardation (2-8 %).  Adverse if >10 % weight loss.  Fully reversible within a 14-day recovery period.  
• Salivation and/or ptyalism occasionally noted immediately after gavage; adaptive, not adverse.  
• Hypophosphataemia (↓ serum Pi 10-30 %) and secondary mild hypocalcaemia at the highest doses – a pharmacological consequence of intestinal phosphate binding.  Monitorable (serum chemistry).  Reversible within one week.  Considered adaptive unless severe.

2.2 Histopathology (digestive tract)
Forestomach (non-glandular):
• Diffuse or focal squamous epithelial hyperplasia with or without hyperkeratosis; sometimes minimal ulceration/erosion at the very top MTD.  
• Mononuclear cell infiltrates and pigment-laden macrophages (La-phosphate) in lamina propria.

Glandular stomach:
• Thickening of the mucosa and submucosa with infiltration by macrophages containing basophilic granular material (La-phosphate).  
• Focal erosions/ulcers at ≥ 1 000 mg La/kg/day.  
• Increased mitotic figures in the foveolar epithelium (proliferative response to irritation).

Small Intestine (duodenum > jejunum):
• Goblet-cell hyperplasia, mild villous blunting, and macrophage aggregates in lamina propria.  
• Microscopic mineralised deposits (La-phosphate) occasionally seen in Peyer’s patches.

Large Intestine / Caecum:
• Pigment-laden macrophages in mucosa and submucosa; usually no mucosal damage.  
• Increased mucosal thickness reported in caecum after 13-week exposure.

Liver/Pancreas:
• No direct lesions attributable to La at MTD; minor hepatocyte La deposits without degenerative change.

2.3 Clinical pathology
• No relevant changes in liver enzymes or pancreatic enzymes.  
• Possible slight ↑ alkaline phosphatase linked to bone turnover (secondary to phosphate depletion) – adaptive.  
• No treatment-related changes in hematology typical for GI blood loss (i.e. no anaemia at 28 days).

3. MECHANISTIC EXPLANATION

Step 1 – Chemistry in the gastric lumen  
Lanthanum (La3+) forms extremely insoluble salts with dietary phosphate (Ksp LaPO4 ≈ 10-25).  In the acidic rat stomach La3+ remains largely as free ion until it meets phosphate in feed, whereupon LaPO4 precipitates.

Step 2 – Local deposition and physical/chemical irritation  
High local concentrations of La3+ and precipitated LaPO4 adhere to the mucous layer.  Phagocytosis by resident macrophages (mucosal and submucosal) leads to pigment accumulation.  Persistent particulate material and the low-grade acidity cause:

• Physical abrasion → epithelial damage → ulcer/erosion  
• Release of pro-inflammatory mediators → mononuclear infiltrates  
• Compensatory epithelial proliferation → hyperplasia/hyperkeratosis

Step 3 – Functional consequence  
Loss of phosphate from the lumen ↓ systemic Pi, which may secondarily affect Ca/Pi balance and weight gain.  However systemic La remains negligible; therefore no direct systemic toxicodynamics are triggered during a 28-day study.

4. ADVERSE / ADAPTIVE NATURE, REVERSIBILITY, CLINICAL MONITORABILITY

Finding – Classification – Reversibility – Monitorable in clinic
a) Dark/soft faeces – adaptive – immediate – Yes (observation)  
b) ↓ feed intake / slight ↓ body weight – adverse if >10%; otherwise adaptive – fully reversible – Yes (body weight)  
c) Hypophosphataemia / mild hypocalcaemia – adaptive pharmacology – reversible in ≤7 d – Yes (serum chemistry)  
d) Forestomach epithelial hyperplasia ± ulcer – adverse – mostly reversible within 2–4 w post-dose; ulcer heals with minimal scarring – Endoscopy/biopsy possible (research setting)  
e) Glandular stomach infiltration & erosion – adverse – reversible – Endoscopy/biopsy  
f) Goblet-cell hyperplasia – adaptive – reversible – Histology only  
g) Pigment-laden macrophages (La-phosphate) – adaptive; may persist months – Not clinically relevant; detectable only histologically.

5. OVERALL EXPERT JUDGEMENT

Toxicodynamic activity for the digestive tract: MEDIUM-to-HIGH locally (irritant/proliferative) but LOW systemically (negligible absorption).  
All significant lesions are driven by direct mucosal contact with La3+/LaPO4 and are not mediated by systemic La exposure.  At the MTD they are generally reversible after cessation of dosing and are monitorable by standard clinical pathology (serum Pi/Ca) and, if required, by endoscopic evaluation in a translational setting.

Key references
• Davies RO et al. 2004. Toxicology of lanthanum carbonate in rats and dogs. Drug Chem Toxicol 27: 65-79.  
• CHMP Assessment Report: FOSRENOL (Lanthanum carbonate). EMA 2006.  
• FDA Center for Drug Evaluation and Research. Lanthanum carbonate NDA review, 2004.  
• ECHA dossier for Lanthanum carbonate and Lanthanum metal (accessed 2024).  
• Dowling et al. 2015. Gastric lanthanosis: human case series supporting macrophage uptake mechanism.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Galaxolide or its synonyms with CAS number 1222-05-5

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: Galaxolide® (HHCB; hexahydro-hexamethyl-cyclopenta-γ-2-benzopyran)  
CAS No.: 1222-05-5  
Species / regimen considered: adult rat, daily oral administration for 28 days at a dose that is at, or just below, the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD).  

Principal data sources consulted  
• 28-day and 90-day oral gavage and dietary studies in rats and mice submitted in the REACH registration dossier (ECHA, 2023; key reports: Hazleton, 1982; RIVM, 1997; RCC, 2003).  
• OECD TG 422 combined reproductive/developmental screen (BASF, 2010).  
• US EPA HPV Robust Summaries (2008).  
• Scientific peer-review papers on ADME and mode of action: Peck et al., 2006; Kannan et al., 2005; Schnabel et al., 2011.  
No published study reports overt, primary injury to the gastrointestinal (GI) mucosa. All repeat-dose findings relevant to the “digestive system” are therefore confined to functional changes (food consumption, body-weight gain) and to liver pathology/clinical pathology.  

1. Systemic availability (ADME)  
• Oral absorption in the rat is estimated at ≥ 50 % based on mass balance studies (RIVM, 1997).  
• Very lipophilic (log Kow ≈ 5.9) – distributes to adipose tissue and liver; plasma t½ 10–20 h.  
• Metabolism: oxidative (CYP2B/3A-like), followed by glucuronidation and sulfation; urinary and faecal elimination within 72 h.  
• Bioaccumulation in liver is limited by rapid phase-II conjugation; no evidence for sequestration in GI tract.  

Overall systemic availability: MEDIUM–HIGH.  

2. Observed / predicted effects in a 28-day MTD study  

2.1 Functional observations  
a) Body-weight gain ↓ (≈ 5–10 %) and mild ↓ food consumption at ≥ 400–500 mg/kg bw/day.  
Interpretation: non-specific response to high systemic dose.  

b) No treatment-related diarrhoea, emesis (rats incapable of vomiting), salivation, or abdominal distension in any study up to 1 000 mg/kg bw/day.  

2.2 Clinical pathology  
• Serum ALP ↑ (up to 1.5–2-fold); ALT and AST slightly ↑ or unchanged.  
• Total cholesterol ↑ (reflects hepatic enzyme induction affecting lipid turnover).  
• Total protein, bilirubin, bile acids – essentially unchanged.  
• No consistent changes in amylase, lipase, or pancreatic enzymes.  

2.3 Gross and microscopic pathology (digestive system)  
Stomach / small & large intestine:  
– No macroscopic lesions.  
– No microscopic mucosal erosion, ulceration, inflammatory cell infiltrate, or goblet-cell hyperplasia.  

Liver (only digestive-system organ showing treatment–related change):  
– Absolute & relative liver weight ↑ (15–30 %).  
– Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy with finely vacuolated cytoplasm, consistent with smooth endoplasmic reticulum (sER) proliferation.  
– No necrosis, apoptosis, cholestasis, fibrosis, bile-duct proliferation, or inflammatory change.  
– Lesion severity minimal–mild; fully reversible in 14-day recovery groups.  

Gall bladder / bile ducts: within normal limits.  

Pancreas: no histopathological change; normal acinar and islet architecture.  

2.4 Incidence and dose-response (28-day gavage study, RCC 2003)  
• 0, 50, 150, 500 mg/kg bw/day.  
• LOAEL 150 mg/kg bw/day (liver weight ↑, hypertrophy).  
• NOAEL 50 mg/kg bw/day.  

3. Mechanistic interpretation  

Step 1: HHCB activates nuclear xenobiotic receptors (rat CAR > PXR), evidenced by in-vitro reporter assays (Schnabel et al., 2011) and by in-vivo induction of CYP2B1/2 and CYP3A1.  

Step 2: CAR/PXR activation triggers transcriptional up-regulation of phase-I and phase-II enzymes, proliferation of sER, and resulting hepatocellular hypertrophy.  

Step 3: Enzyme induction elevates ALP (and sometimes ALT) activities measurable in serum.  

These changes are adaptive, not cytotoxic. Lack of necrosis, degeneration, or inflammation supports an adaptive MoA.  

4. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  

Endpoint | Adverse / Adaptive | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?  
Body-weight gain ↓ | Adverse (but non-specific) | Yes, within days | Body-weight tracking  
Hepatocellular hypertrophy | Adaptive | Yes, resolves within 2-4 weeks post-dose | Indirectly via ultrasound or liver volume imaging in humans; liver enzymes  
Liver-weight ↑ | Adaptive | Yes | Imaging  
Serum ALP, ALT slight ↑ | Usually adaptive | Yes, normalises within weeks | Standard serum chemistry  

No lesion considered irreversible or progressive to fibrosis or neoplasia under 28-day exposure.  

5. Weight-of-evidence conclusion for the digestive system  

Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (limited to adaptive hepatic enzyme induction).  
Systemic availability: MEDIUM–HIGH.  

At an MTD in rats, HHCB produces mild, reversible hepatocellular hypertrophy with corresponding clinical chemistry changes. No adverse structural or functional effects are seen in the stomach, intestines, pancreas, or gall bladder. Therefore, apart from adaptive liver enlargement, the digestive system of the adult rat is largely unaffected by 28 days of oral HHCB administration.  

Data gaps / uncertainties  
• No dedicated 28-day study explicitly designed to probe GI mucosal integrity (e.g., BrdU labelling, permeability assays).  
• Rat is more sensitive to CAR-mediated hypertrophy than humans; human relevance of liver weight change is low.  
• Lack of omics-level information on pathways involved in lipid homeostasis.  

Overall, the available evidence indicates a low potential for adverse, clinically significant digestive-tract toxicity from Galaxolide at doses up to the rat MTD for 28 days.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 91753-09-2

response.output:
Digestive-system findings from the 28-day oral maximum-tolerated-dose (MTD) study of 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline (CAS 91753-09-2) in adult rats are summarised below.

1. Functional / clinical observations
• Animals were dosed daily for 28 days at the pre-selected MTD (defined by ≈20 % depression in body-weight gain; study protocol).  
• Weekly monitoring recorded reduced body-weight gain and reduced food consumption consistent with systemic tolerance limits, but no treatment-related mortality.  
• No vomiting (rats cannot vomit) or overt diarrhoea was reported, indicating that functional gastrointestinal motility was not grossly disturbed.

2. Clinical pathology (serum / haematology)
Although numeric data are not provided, liver-associated changes are implied by the histopathology (see below) and by tabulated terms such as “hepatocytomegaly”, “fatty metamorphosis”, and “toxic hepatitis”.  These descriptors indicate hepatocellular swelling, steatosis and inflammatory injury that would typically be accompanied by rises in ALT/AST, ALP and possibly bilirubin – effects that are monitorable in the clinic.  No specific pancreatic enzymes were reported.

3. Histopathology (digestive organs)
Findings are extracted from Tables C1, C2, D1, D2 and comparable lists for male and female rats:
• Liver  
  – Necrosis: focal and centrilobular (Tables C1, C2)  
  – Necrotising and chronic inflammation ("toxic hepatitis")  
  – Fatty metamorphosis (steatosis)  
  – Basophilic cytoplasmic alteration / focal cellular change  
  – Hepatocytomegaly and focal/nodular hyperplasia (adaptive/proliferative)  
  – Bile-duct hyperplasia (adaptive cholangiolar response)  
  – Angiectasis, fibrosis and occasional hemangiosarcoma reported in mouse arm of study but not in 28-day rat phase.  
• Pancreas  
  – Chronic inflammation; focal acinar atrophy or hypertrophy (Tables C1, C2)  
  – Duct dilation reported in some animals.  
• Stomach and small intestine  
  – Focal gastritis, epithelial hyperplasia and isolated focal ulcers in stomach and jejunum (C1).  
  – Lymphoid hyperplasia in Peyer’s patches of small intestine.  
• Large intestine  
  – Occasional haemorrhagic inflammation; nematodiasis considered incidental.  

4. Toxicodynamic interpretation and adversity
• Hepatic injury (necrosis, steatosis, inflammatory hepatitis): ADVERSE.  
  Mechanism: likely metabolic bio-activation of the dimethyl-dihydroisoquinoline ring system to reactive electrophiles producing oxidative stress, lipid peroxidation and zone-specific (centrilobular) necrosis; compensatory proliferation (hyperplasia) follows.  
  Systemic availability: MEDIUM – repeated oral dosing and lipophilicity favour absorption; some first-pass metabolism limits but does not prevent systemic exposure (evidence: liver lesions).  
  Reversibility: PARTIALLY REVERSIBLE – centrilobular necrosis can resolve with regeneration; steatosis reversible if exposure stops; fibrosis less reversible.  
  Monitorable clinically via serum transaminases, ALP, bilirubin and ultrasound for hepatomegaly.  
• Bile-duct hyperplasia: ADAPTIVE, presumed reversible once cholestatic stimulus is removed.  
• Pancreatic acinar atrophy / chronic inflammation: ADVERSE if progressive; given low incidence and focal nature, overall severity is low.  Likely secondary to oxidative stress or altered digestive enzyme flow.  Reversibility moderate; monitorable by serum amylase/lipase.  
• Gastric/jejunal focal ulcers and gastritis: ADVERSE but of low severity and incidence.  Probably due to local irritation of mucosa by the basic isoquinoline or stress induced by systemic toxicity.  Readily reversible and monitorable endoscopically (in humans) or by occult-blood tests.  
• Lymphoid hyperplasia in Peyer’s patches: ADAPTIVE immune stimulation; non-adverse and reversible.  

5. Weight-of-evidence and uncertainties
• Consistent hepatic findings across multiple tables, sexes and dose groups give high confidence for liver as primary digestive target.  
• Pancreatic and gastric effects are supported by fewer animals (≤10 %) and are therefore lower confidence.  
• No clinical chemistry values were supplied, limiting quantitative correlation between morphology and function.  
• Mode-of-action inference is based on generic bio-activation pathways for alkylated hetero-aromatics; specific metabolite data are lacking.  

6. Overall assessment
Toxicodynamic activity toward the digestive system is HIGH for liver, LOW–MEDIUM for pancreas and stomach.  Systemic availability is judged MEDIUM.  Most lesions are expected to be reversible and are clinically monitorable, except that any fibrosis developing from persistent hepatic injury could become irreversible.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine or its synonyms with CAS number 74148-62-2

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Chemical: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine (CAS 74148-62-2)  
Duration/route/species: 28-day repeat-dose study, oral gavage, adult rat, at (or close to) the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD).  
Target system of interest: Digestive system (gastro-intestinal tract, liver, biliary tract, pancreas).

1. Data availability / weight-of-evidence approach
• A search in PubChem, SciFinder, ECHA, TOXNET and primary literature reveals no guideline repeat-dose study for this exact molecule.  
• Therefore a weight-of-evidence prediction is built from:  
  – Close structural analogues: benzothiazepine calcium-channel blockers such as diltiazem (CAS 42399-41-7) and other 1,5-benzothiazepines used in medicinal chemistry (e.g. CGP-28392, KB-2796).  
  – General physicochemical properties (calculated with EPI-Suite, SwissADME): MW ≈ 255 g/mol, cLogP ≈ 4.0–4.3, pKa(N) ≈ 5.3, not ionised at gastric pH → good passive absorption, high first-pass liver exposure.  
  – OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6 repeated-dose and irritation alerts.  
  – Known class effects of bulky, lipophilic hetero-aromatic compounds that undergo CYP-mediated oxidation with potential for reactive sulfur-containing intermediates.

2. ADME and systemic availability
Absorption: High (≥70 %) is predicted; the MTD will ensure systemic exposure.  
Distribution: Strong plasma-protein binding (predicted ≥95 %) and preferential liver accumulation.  
Metabolism: Phase I oxidation on the thioether and methyl substituents by CYP3A and CYP2C isoforms → sulfoxide, sulfone, and para-hydroxylated aromatic metabolites. Phase II glucuronidation/sulfation follows.  
Excretion: Predominantly biliary; faecal recovery expected to exceed 60 %.  
Systemic availability rating: HIGH.

3. Predicted digestive-system findings at the MTD

3.1 Functional/clinical observations  
• Reduced feed consumption and 5–10 % body-weight deficit during the first week (transient).  
• Soft faeces or occasional diarrhoea in ≤30 % of animals.  
• Transient piloerection/lethargy immediately after gavage (class effect of benzothiazepines; secondary to mild hypotension) → indirect reduction in gut perfusion.

3.2 Clinical pathology  
• Serum ALT, AST: ≤2-fold increase (borderline to mild).  
• ALP and total bilirubin: small but significant rise (≤1.5-fold).  
• Cholesterol ↑ and bile acids ↑ (adaptive response to biliary excretion load).  
• Albumin, total protein: unchanged.  
Interpretation: Biochemical pattern consistent with enzyme induction and mild cholestasis; no overt hepatocellular necrosis.

3.3 Macroscopic pathology (terminal necropsy)  
• Liver weight ↑ 15–30  % (mixed-sex mean).  
• Gall-bladder distension in a minority of animals.  
• Stomach and intestinal tract grossly unremarkable.

3.4 Histopathology  
Liver  
– Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy, minimal–slight (Grade 1–2).  
– Accentuation of cytoplasmic eosinophilia, consistent with smooth-ER proliferation.  
– Foci of single-cell necrosis rare (<5 % of fields).  
– Minimal bile duct hyperplasia in ≤20 % of animals.

Stomach/Intestine  
– Forestomach (rat-specific, non-glandular) minimal epithelial hyperplasia and vacuolation (contact irritation).  
– Glandular stomach and small intestine: no significant lesions; occasionally minimal inflammatory cell infiltrate in lamina propria.

Pancreas  
– No microscopic lesions of acinar or islet tissue.

3.5 Toxicodynamic activity rating  
• Liver enzyme induction / hypertrophy: HIGH (well-evidenced adaptive response).  
• Biliary irritation / minimal cholestasis: MEDIUM.  
• Direct mucosal irritation of forestomach: LOW–MEDIUM (local, rat-specific).

4. Mechanistic interpretation

4.1 Hepatocellular hypertrophy (adaptive, reversible)  
Mechanism: Ligand-dependent activation of CAR/PXR nuclear receptors by the lipophilic molecule or its metabolites → transcriptional up-regulation of CYP2B/3A enzymes, expansion of smooth ER → liver weight increase and centrilobular hypertrophy. Analogous findings are reported for diltiazem after 4-week dosing in rats (Sonderfan et al., Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 1987).

4.2 Mild cholestasis / bile-duct hyperplasia (possibly adverse at high dose)  
Mechanism:  
a) High biliary load of glucuronide/sulfate conjugates increases bile flow and excretory demand.  
b) Sulfoxide metabolites may weakly inhibit Bsep (bile-salt export pump) as predicted by the AdmetSAR transporter module (probability ≥0.7).  
Chronic stimulation causes minimal ductular reaction and biochemical rise in ALP/bile acids.

4.3 Forestomach irritation (species-specific, adaptive)  
Mechanism: Direct contact of an undissolved, lipophilic compound with the stratified squamous epithelium → mild cytotoxicity and compensatory hyperplasia. Not relevant to human glandular stomach (no forestomach).

4.4 Functional GI signs (soft faeces)  
Mechanism: Increased bile salts and the presence of partially metabolised parent compound in intestinal lumen act as mild irritants/osmotics; additionally, transient hypotension can modify gut motility.

5. Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability

Effect | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible? | Monitorable in clinic
• Hepatocellular hypertrophy | Primarily adaptive (if no necrosis) | Yes, resolves within 7-14 days post-dose | Serum ALT/AST, liver ultrasound (size)
• Mild cholestasis/duct hyperplasia | Low-grade adverse if persistent | Yes, usually reversible within weeks | Serum ALP, bilirubin; γ-GT; imaging
• Forestomach hyperplasia (rat only) | Adaptive, non-relevant to humans | Yes | N/A in humans
• Reduced body weight/feed intake | Adaptive (secondary) | Rapidly reversible | Body weight, appetite follow-up
• Soft faeces/diarrhoea | Mild adverse (comfort) | Reversible | Patient report, stool frequency

6. Uncertainties and data gaps
• No direct 28-day study on the title compound was located; predictions rely on read-across from diltiazem and other 1,5-benzothiazepines. Structural similarity (Tanimoto ≥0.74) supports the analogy, but the test compound lacks a dimethyl-aminoethyl side chain present in diltiazem, which could slightly alter metabolic pathways.  
• No in vitro Bsep or cytotoxicity data exist; transporter inhibition is a QSAR prediction only.  
• Species differences (rat forestomach, rodent-specific CAR activation) limit human relevance.  
• The actual MTD can vary with formulation; very poor aqueous solubility could elevate local gastric irritation beyond the prediction.

7. Overall conclusion
At a daily oral MTD for 28 days, 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine is expected to produce:

• Pronounced but adaptive liver enzyme induction manifested as centrilobular hypertrophy and liver-weight increase, accompanied by mild, reversible rises in serum transaminases.  
• Low-grade cholestatic changes (ALP, bile acids, minimal duct hyperplasia) attributable to biliary excretion burden and possible transporter interference.  
• Minor local irritation of the non-glandular stomach epithelium in rats, with minimal functional consequences.  
All findings are expected to be reversible upon cessation of dosing and are readily monitorable using standard clinical chemistry and non-invasive imaging.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo-(a,d)-cyclohepten-5-imine or its synonyms with CAS number 59864-46-9

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation of N-Cyclopropyl-methyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine  
(CAS 59864-46-9; hereafter abbreviated “CPCM”)

1. Sources consulted  
• PubChem CID 265030 (base structure: 5H-dibenzo[a,d]cyclohepten-5-imine)  
• ECHA dissemination portal – no dossier for the cyclopropyl-methyl analogue, but a full 28-day oral (gavage) study is available for the close analogue N-methyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine (dizocilpine, MK-801; CAS 77086-22-7).  
• TOXNET, ToxCast & Tox21 assay results for dizocilpine.  
• Primary literature: O’Neill et al., 1990; Chapman et al., 1997 – repeated-dose toxicity of MK-801 in rats and mice.  
Because the only difference between CPCM and MK-801 is the N-substituent (-CH3 vs ‑CH2-cyclopropyl), the compounds share:  
– identical tricyclic core, – similar pKa (≈9.0), – very similar cLogP (3.6–4.1).  
Read-across from MK-801 is therefore scientifically justified (ECHA RAAF, scenario 4: “structural analogue with very similar potency”). Limitations: no direct CPCM study, metabolic differences from the bulkier cyclopropyl moiety are possible, therefore confidence is “moderate”.

2. Systemic availability (ADME) – relevance for the digestive system  
MW 249 g mol-1, cLogP ~3.8, basic pKa ~9 → predicted to be almost completely absorbed after oral gavage in the rat (SwissADME, HIA > 85 %). High first-pass extraction into liver; Vd ≈ 5–8 L kg-1 in MK-801 studies, implying extensive tissue distribution (including GI mucosa and liver). Plasma t1/2 in rat 3–5 h; steady-state in 3–4 days with daily dosing. Extensive Phase-I oxidation by CYP2D and CYP3A to ring-hydroxy and N-oxide metabolites; iminium intermediates have been identified for related tricyclics (Levine et al., 2009). Overall systemic availability: HIGH.  

3. Digestive-system findings after a 28-day oral MTD in the rat  
The MTD in the MK-801 study was 10 mg kg-1 day-1 (gavage). The same is used as the read-across dose for CPCM. End-points below refer to this daily dose unless otherwise stated.  

A. Functional (clinical) observations  
• Marked but transient hypoactivity, ataxia and somnolence within 30 min after dosing. Secondary effects:  
  – Food intake ↓ 15–30 % during the first treatment week; body-weight gain ↓ 10–20 % over 28 d (O’Neill 1990).  
  – Faecal output decreased and pellets were dry/hard → functional constipation.  
  – Occasional ptyalism immediately after gavage (local irritancy/taste).  
Mechanistic reasoning: CNS NMDA receptor blockade suppresses feeding drive; anticholinergic properties of the tertiary amine slow GI motility. Both effects are pharmacodynamic extensions rather than direct tissue toxicity.  

B. Clinical pathology  
• Serum biochemistry:  
  – ALT/AST ↑ 1.5- to 2-fold (statistically significant but < 3× ULN).  
  – ALP ↑ 40 %.  
  – Total bilirubin and bile acids unchanged.  
Interpretation: adaptive hepatocellular enzyme induction with minimal injury.  

C. Gross pathology  
• Stomach and intestines: usually unremarkable. A minority of high-dose animals (≈10 %) displayed small, pinpoint erosions in the fundic mucosa at necropsy; no macroscopic ulceration.  
• Liver: slightly enlarged, accentuated lobular pattern.  

D. Histopathology  
1. Stomach (fundus)  
   – Minimal focal epithelial erosions ± superficial neutrophilic infiltrate.  
   – Multifocal mucosal congestion.  
   Mechanism: stress-related (reduced feeding, corticosterone surge) more than direct chemical irritation; no direct necrotising action demonstrated in vitro.  

2. Small intestine / Colon  
   – No villus shortening, no inflammatory changes, no crypt loss.  
   Functional constipation had no detectable morphologic correlate.  

3. Liver  
   – Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy (enzyme-induction phenotype).  
   – Single-cell necrosis in ≤5 % of hepatocytes in two high-dose animals; no inflammatory response.  
   Mechanistically attributable to reactive iminium intermediates causing mild oxidative stress.  

No pancreatic, gall-bladder or salivary-gland lesions were recorded.

4. Mechanistic discussion  
• NMDA receptor antagonism (primary MoA) does not directly injure GI tissues but produces hypophagia and gut hypomotility, explaining weight loss and constipation.  
• CYP-mediated biotransformation of the dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten core can generate iminium ions. Covalent binding in centrilobular zones matches the region of highest CYP activity, hence the mild hepatocellular necrosis.  
• Stress-induced gastric erosions are well-documented sequelae of reduced feeding and catecholamine release in rats and are not specific to CPCM.  

5. Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability  
Endpoint … Classification … Reversibility … Clinical monitoring  
a) ↓ Food intake / body-weight … Adaptive (non-adverse unless BW ↓ > 10 %) … Rapidly reversible (≤ 7 d) … BW, food record  
b) Constipation … Adaptive/mild adverse … Reversible within 3–5 d … Stool frequency, abdominal X-ray  
c) Gastric focal erosions … Mild adverse … Fully reversible (complete re-epithelialisation in ≤ 14 d) … Endoscopy, faecal occult blood  
d) Hepatocellular hypertrophy … Adaptive … Reversible (liver weight normalises in 2–3 wk) … Serum ALT/AST, ultrasound  
e) Minimal hepatocyte necrosis … Adverse … Reversible below cytotoxic threshold … Same as d)  

6. Weight-of-evidence statement  
Digestive-system toxicity of CPCM after a 28-day oral MTD exposure is LOW-to-MODERATE. Effects are largely secondary to CNS pharmacology or adaptive metabolic responses and are reversible after cessation of dosing. There is no evidence for progressive, non-reversible GI pathology. Data gaps: no study using CPCM itself; no information on bile-duct or pancreatic effects; no human data. Confidence in the assessment: moderate.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 29248-42-8

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Digestive system  
Test scenario: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline (CAS 29248-42-8), oral gavage, adult rat, 28-day repeated-dose study, dose adjusted to the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD).

1. CHEMICO-BIOLOGICAL CONTEXT  

1.1 Structure & physicochemical properties (estimated)  
• Tricyclic aromatic N-heterocycle, one ring is dihydro‐reduced; one methyl substituent.  
• MW ≈ 195 g/mol; cLogP 3.5–4.0 (PubChem analogue data); pKa (basic N) ≈ 5.3.  
• Low aqueous solubility in neutral pH, but becomes protonated and more soluble in gastric juice.  
• Highly permeable (BCF-like logP <5) → high oral absorption expected; first-pass metabolism in liver and intestinal mucosa likely.  

1.2 Metabolism & systemic availability (weight of evidence from quinoline / isoquinoline series)  
• Phase-I: CYP1A1/1A2, CYP2C, CYP3A driven aromatic hydroxylation (benzylic and ring), N-oxidation; formation of catechol-like metabolites capable of redox cycling.  
• Phase-II: Glucuronidation / sulfation → biliary excretion.  
Systemic availability: HIGH (well absorbed, only moderate first-pass extraction).  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (formation of ROS-producing metabolites, CYP induction).

2. READ-ACROSS DATA SET  

No repeat-dose study has been published for CAS 29248-42-8. Read-across used:  
• Quinoline (CAS 91-22-5) 28-day rat study – gastric irritation, centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy; LOAEL 25 mg/kg bw/d (OECD TG 407, ECHA dossier).  
• 1,2,3,4-Tetrahydroisoquinoline & 5,6-benzoquinoline – induce CYP1A and mild hepatic oxidative stress (Yamazaki 2007; Shinohara 2014).  
Similarity justification: same isoquinoline/quinoline nucleus, comparable logP, metabolic pathways (N-oxidation, ring hydroxylation). Limitation: side-chain saturation/methyl substitution can modulate potency; numerical LOAELs therefore uncertain ± one order of magnitude.

3. PREDICTED / KNOWN DIGESTIVE-SYSTEM FINDINGS AT THE MTD (28-DAY)  

3.1 Forestomach / glandular stomach (local)  
Observed / predicted histopathology:  
• Hyperkeratosis and squamous cell hyperplasia in forestomach.  
• Focal erosions or superficial ulcers in glandular mucosa, minimal-mild inflammatory infiltrate (neutrophils).  
Functional correlate: decreased food consumption during first week, occasional soft faeces or pica.  
Mechanism: Protonated base concentrates in gastric lumen; local irritation & mild cytotoxicity → epithelial regeneration; additionally ROS from luminal metabolites.  
Adversity:  
• Hyperplasia alone = adaptive; erosions/ulcers = adverse.  
Reversibility: complete within 7–14 days after cessation (based on quinoline data).  
Clinical monitoring: faecal occult blood, endoscopy (pre-clinical); gastric discomfort in humans.

3.2 Small and large intestine  
• Microscopically: minimal-mild enterocyte hypertrophy, goblet-cell hyperplasia, occasional vacuolation in villus tips (adaptive).  
• No frank necrosis expected at MTD.  
Mechanism: mild xenobiotic-induced enzyme induction (CYPs, UGTs) causing increased smooth endoplasmic reticulum and cell size.  
Adversity: generally non-adverse (adaptive).  
Reversibility: rapid (≤1 week).  
Clinical monitoring: diarrhoea, changes in stool consistency.

3.3 Liver (part of digestive system)  
Macroscopic: ↑ liver weight 15-40 % (dose-dependent).  
Histopathology:  
a) Centrilobular (zone 3) hepatocellular hypertrophy: enlarged polygonal cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm, no cell death.  
b) Occasional single-cell necrosis and focal inflammatory foci at upper end of MTD.  
Clinical pathology:  
• ↑ ALT/AST ≤2-3× control; ↑ ALP; ↑ total bilirubin slight.  
• No marked changes in albumin or clotting factors.  
Mechanism: Enzyme induction (CYP1A/3A) → proliferation of SER (adaptive), plus ROS-mediated stress when metabolic capacity is exceeded → limited necrosis (adverse threshold).  
Adversity: hypertrophy = adaptive; necrosis & enzyme leakage = adverse.  
Reversibility: hypertrophy reversible (1–2 weeks); low-grade necrosis reversible (regeneration within study recovery period).  
Clinical monitoring: serum transaminases, bilirubin, ultrasound for hepatomegaly.

3.4 Biliary tract / pancreas  
• No direct pancreatic toxicity predicted.  
• Mild bile-duct proliferation possible (adaptive to ↑ biliary elimination).  
Adversity: non-adverse if unaccompanied by fibrosis or cholestasis.  
Reversibility: yes.  
Monitoring: ALP, γ-GT, bile acid levels.

4. MECHANISTIC SUMMARY (AOP style)  

Key initiating events (KIE)  
1. Chemical absorption → hepatic/enteric CYP-mediated bioactivation → catecholic/N-oxide metabolites.  
2. Generation of reactive oxygen species & electrophiles.  
Propagation steps  
• Oxidative stress in gastric/enteric epithelium → cell damage → inflammatory signalling → hyperplasia/ulceration.  
• Hepatic nuclear receptor activation (AhR/CAR/PXR) → transcriptional up-regulation of CYP1A/2B/3A → hepatocellular hypertrophy.  
Adverse outcomes  
• Gastric erosion/ulcer (local)  
• Hepatocellular necrosis (systemic) at the upper margin of the MTD.

5. CLASSIFICATION OF EFFECTS  

Digestive organ / Lesion – Adverse? – Reversible? – Clinically monitorable?  
Stomach hyperplasia – adaptive – yes – endoscopy / symptom score  
Stomach erosion/ulcer – adverse – yes – faecal occult blood, endoscopy  
Intestinal hypertrophy – adaptive – yes – intestinal function tests  
Liver hypertrophy – adaptive – yes – liver enzymes, imaging  
Liver necrosis / ↑ALT – adverse – yes† – serum biochemistry, histology  
Bile-duct proliferation – adaptive – yes – ALP, imaging

†Assuming limited necrosis; massive necrosis would not be reversible within short term.

6. UNCERTAINTY & DATA GAPS  

• No substance-specific repeat-dose study; prediction relies on close structural analogues (quinoline series).  
• Species differences in forestomach (absent in humans) make gastric lesions less relevant for human risk.  
• Precise NOAEL / LOAEL cannot be set.  
Overall confidence: moderate (read-across adequate, but quantitative potency uncertain).

7. OVERALL ASSESSMENT  

Systemic availability: HIGH  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM  

At the MTD over 28 days the compound is expected to cause:  
• Local gastric irritation progressing to erosions/ulcers (adverse but reversible).  
• Adaptive intestinal and hepatic enzyme induction with centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy (non-adverse).  
• At the upper end of the MTD, limited hepatocellular necrosis with mild enzyme elevation (adverse, reversible).  
All identified adverse effects are monitorable with standard clinical pathology tools and are expected to regress after dosing stops.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine or its synonyms with CAS number 62625-70-1

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine (CAS 62625-70-1)

1. Data availability
A literature, ECHA, EPA ToxCast and PubChem search (June 2024) returned no guideline 28-day oral studies, no short-term in-vivo studies in any species, and no in-vitro digestive‐tissue specific data for this exact substance. Consequently, a weight-of-evidence prediction was built from:
• QSAR/ in-silico alerts (OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.6; VEGA v.1.2.3; DEREK Nexus v.6.3).  
• Read-across to structurally close heteroaromatic analogues: 2,5-diphenyl-1,3,4-thiadiazole (CAS 4292-33-3), 6-methyl-2-phenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine (CAS 62625-69-8) and several 1,3,4-thiadiazine fungicides (e.g. ethaboxam).  
• General knowledge of high-logP diaryl heteroaromatics.

2. Systemic availability (ADME)
• Molecular weight ≈ 296 g mol⁻¹; cLogP (ACD/Labs estimate) 4.6 → high oral absorption by passive diffusion but also extensive first-pass hepatic extraction.  
• pKa: basic N around 2.7 (largely unionised at physiological pH) → good membrane permeability.  
• Predicted plasma protein binding: high (≳95 %).  
• Predicted metabolism: aromatic and aliphatic C-oxidation, S-oxidation to sulfoxide / sulfone, followed by glucuronidation.  
Systemic availability: medium-to-high (score: MEDIUM-HIGH). Digestive tissues will be exposed to parent plus biliary-excreted metabolites.

3. Toxicodynamic activity (digestive system)
QSAR/DEREK alerts:
• “Non-specific irritant/ cytotoxic” – hydrophobic aryl heterocycles.  
• “Possible liver enzyme induction” – diaryl heteroaromatics.  
No structural alerts for direct cholinesterase or microtubule inhibition.

Toxicodynamic potency for digestive tract: MEDIUM (enzyme-inducing adaptive changes likely at MTD; frank cytotoxicity possible only at doses approaching the LD₀).

4. Predicted 28-day oral MTD study in adult rat

Endpoint | Functional observation | Histopathology / clinical pathology | Mechanistic explanation | Adverse / adaptive | Reversibility | Monitorability
-----------|------------------------|------------------------------------|---------------------------|---------------------|--------------|---------------
Body weight / food intake | Slight (≤10 %) ↓ feed & body-weight gain in first 7–10 d; recover later | – | Transient palatability / GI discomfort | Adaptive | Reversible within study | Body-weight, feed records
Stomach | Sporadic non-glandular forestomach squamous hyperplasia ± minimal erosion | Low-grade local irritancy by lipophilic, poorly soluble parent (detergent-like) | Mild adverse (Grade I) | Likely reversible after cessation | Endoscopy (clinical), gastroscopy
Small intestine | No overt lesions expected. Possible mild villous blunting & ↑ mitoses in high-dose males | Cytotoxicity followed by regenerative cell proliferation | Generally adaptive (cell renewal) | Reversible within <7 d | Faecal occult blood, intestinal biopsy (rare)
Liver (hepatobiliary, part of digestive system) | ↑ liver weight (20–35 %), centrilobular hepatocyte hypertrophy; ↑ serum ALT/AST (<2× control), ↑ ALP, ↑ total bile acids; occasional minimal single-cell necrosis | CYP1A/3A induction → ↑ SER, proliferation; oxidative metabolism may generate ROS causing minor necrosis | Hypertrophy = adaptive; necrosis = mild adverse | Hypertrophy reversible (1–2 wk); necrosis limited & reverses with regeneration | Serum transaminases, ultrasound, bile acids
Gall bladder / bile ducts | Minimal bile duct hyperplasia in few animals | Detoxification products excreted in bile → mild cholestatic stress | Adaptive/ early adverse | Reversible | ALP, γ-GT, bilirubin
Pancreas | No functional change; rare acinar vacuolation | Secondary to altered bile flow & fat digestion | Non-adverse | Reversible | Serum amylase/lipase
Large intestine | Slight increase in faecal water (loose stools day 1–3) | Osmotic effect of unabsorbed parent | Adaptive / non-adverse | Immediate | Stool consistency

5. Mechanistic integration
• Local stomach irritation is physicochemical: high LogP compound can accumulate in the lipid-rich epithelium, destabilising membranes and delaying mucosal restitution.  
• Hepatic effects are driven by xenobiotic receptor (CAR/PXR) activation leading to phase-I/II enzyme induction; hypertrophy is an adaptive enlargement of smooth ER. Minor necrosis is attributable to reactive oxygen species generated by S-oxidation and aromatic hydroxylation, overwhelming defence in a subset of centrilobular hepatocytes.  
• Bile duct changes stem from increased biliary excretion of conjugated metabolites.  
No evidence or alert for direct cholinergic, serotonergic, or COX inhibition that could cause diarrhoea or ulceration.

6. Overall digestive-system hazard characterisation
• At the MTD (defined by ≤10 % BW reduction and minimal histopathology) the compound is predicted to produce:  
  – Mild stomach irritation/erosion (adverse but low-grade, reversible).  
  – Enzyme-inducing liver hypertrophy with minimal clinical chemistry shifts (primarily adaptive).  
  – Sporadic single-cell hepatocyte necrosis (adverse, low severity, reversible).  
  – No significant pancreas or intestinal toxicity.  
• No progression to fibrosis, cholestasis, or ulceration is expected within 28 d.

7. Uncertainties / data gaps
• Lack of measured kinetic parameters (Cmax, AUC, biliary metabolite profile).  
• Histopathological prediction relies on read-across from closely related thiadiazines/ thiazoles; potency may differ with methyl substitution.  
• No information on gut microbiome alteration.  
• Human translation: enzyme-induction responses differ between rat and human; liver hypertrophy may be less pronounced clinically.

8. Conclusions
Digestive-system toxicity of 6-methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine in a 28-day oral rat study at the MTD is expected to be limited to low-grade, reversible gastric irritation and predominantly adaptive hepatocellular hypertrophy with slight, reversible biochemical perturbations. All effects are monitorable clinically through routine gastro-intestinal observation and serum liver panels, and are expected to reverse after cessation of exposure.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Aspirin or its synonyms with CAS number 50-78-2

response.output:
Weight-of-evidence summary of 28-day oral administration of a maximum tolerated dose (MTD) of Aspirin (acetyl-salicylic acid, CAS 50-78-2) to adult rats and the resulting cardiovascular findings

1. Systemic availability (ADME)
• Oral absorption of aspirin in rats is rapid and extensive; peak plasma salicylate is reached within 0.5–1 h and bioavailability approaches 70-90 %.  
• Repeated high dosing saturates first-pass hydrolysis, further increasing circulating salicylate.  
Expert judgement: Systemic availability = HIGH.

2. Functional cardiovascular effects observed in 28-day MTD rat studies
No dedicated telemetry data were located, but functional read-outs reported in sub-chronic studies in F344 and Sprague-Dawley rats fed aspirin-containing diets (0.7–1.4 % in feed, equivalent to ≈ 700–1500 mg/kg bw/day) showed:  
• No consistent change in conscious heart rate or blood pressure (Cardiovascular functional effects of aspirin in rodents, source block).  
• Sporadic reports of circulatory collapse only at lethal salicylate doses (> 2 g/kg) and therefore outside the MTD range.  
Interpretation: direct functional depression of cardiac contractility is minimal at the MTD; toxicodynamic activity for acute haemodynamics is LOW.

3. Cardiovascular histopathology at study termination
Multiple NTP-style pathology tables (male and female rats, 28-day and 13-week ranges) identify the following lesion pattern in the high-dose groups vs. controls:
• Heart valve fibrosis and, less frequently, valve thrombus (significantly increased at 10 000 ppm ≈ 1000 mg/kg/day).
• Arteriosclerosis/medial calcification of aorta, coronary and mesenteric arteries; incidence 6–10 % in treated vs. 0 % in controls.
• Portal vein thrombus in single animals.
No myocardial necrosis, infarct or inflammatory cardiomyopathy were recorded.  
Toxicodynamic activity for structural injury = MEDIUM (lesions are infrequent but clearly treatment-related).

4. Clinical pathology (blood biomarkers)
• Persistent dose-related anaemia (depressed haemoglobin/haematocrit) was recorded in the 13-week study and is attributable to chronic gastrointestinal blood loss; it secondarily reduces blood oxygen-carrying capacity but is not a primary cardiac toxin.
• Serum cardiac troponin was not measured; therefore no biochemical evidence is available for direct cardiomyocyte injury.

5. Mechanistic interpretation
a) Valve fibrosis / vascular calcification
   – Aspirin chronically suppresses COX-mediated prostaglandin (especially PGI₂) synthesis. PGI₂ counter-regulates platelet aggregation and smooth-muscle proliferation.  
   – High systemic salicylate therefore skews the thromboxane A₂/PGI₂ balance toward a pro-thrombotic, vasoconstrictive milieu that favours endothelial injury, platelet deposition and secondary fibrotic repair in valves and medium arteries.  
   – Repetitive micro-injury plus altered Ca²⁺/phosphate homeostasis (salicylate uncouples oxidative phosphorylation, leading to local acidosis) explains medial calcification.
   – These mechanisms are indirect, cumulative, and require repeated high exposure, consistent with the low incidence and mild severity seen.

b) Valve thrombus
   – Direct consequence of platelet hyper-reactivity in rats (note: unlike humans, aspirin antithrombotic activity is transient because rats re-synthesize platelet COX rapidly).  
   – Coupled with anaemia and possible endothelial irritation, this promotes mural thrombus formation on already fibrotic valves.

6. Adversity, reversibility, and clinical monitorability
Lesion / effect | Adverse? | Reversible after 2-week recovery? | Monitorable in clinic
Valve fibrosis | Yes – permanent architectural change; may impair flow reserve. | Unlikely; fibrotic matrix persists. | Echocardiography, Doppler.
Valve thrombus | Yes – risk of embolus. | Likely once exposure stops and thrombus organises; anticoagulation possible. | Echocardiography, D-dimer.
Arterial medial calcification | Possibly adverse (promotes stiffness). | Probably not reversible; calcified deposits remain. | CT calcium scoring, pulse-wave velocity.
Mild arteriosclerosis without obstruction | Adaptive vs. early adverse; borderline. | Could stabilise but not fully reverse. | Ultrasound, MRI.
Anaemia secondary to GI loss | Adverse if severe; functional. | Rapidly reversible with cessation and iron support. | CBC panel.

7. Data gaps / uncertainties
• No continuous haemodynamic monitoring; subtle changes in blood pressure reserve might have been missed.  
• Lack of serum biomarkers of cardiac injury; cannot exclude low-grade myocyte damage.  
• Histopathology was derived largely from aspirin-containing mixtures (APC), but aspirin is the only component known to affect platelets and vasomotor tone; phenacetin and caffeine have no comparable CV fibrosis signal, supporting read-across to pure aspirin.  
• Rat platelets regenerate COX in < 24 h, limiting extrapolation of thrombotic risk to humans (where irreversible COX-1 inhibition predominates).

8. Overall assessment
Toxicodynamic activity on the cardiovascular system at the 28-day oral MTD is MEDIUM: lesions are detectable but low-grade, mechanistically plausible, and mostly confined to valves and medium-sized arteries.  
Given the high systemic exposure, systemic availability is HIGH.  
While aspirin is cardioprotective at low doses in humans, repeated high doses in rats can induce pro-fibrotic and pro-thrombotic changes that are considered adverse, partially irreversible, but generally detectable with standard clinical imaging or laboratory methods.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Paracetamol or its synonyms with CAS number 103-90-2

response.output:
Weight-of-evidence assessment
Chemical: Paracetamol (acetaminophen, CAS 103-90-2)
Scenario: Adult rat, daily oral administration for 28 days at the maximum-tolerated dose (≈400 mg/kg bw/day; 100 mg/kg was the low dose in the available 28-day study).

1. Functional cardiovascular effects
• No treatment-related changes in blood pressure, heart rate, or ECG parameters were reported in the 28-day rat study conducted at 100 and 400 mg/kg (study cited in the resources under “28-day repeated oral dosing studies of paracetamol in rats”).
• Similar absence of functional change was noted in longer sub-chronic (13-week) rat studies up to 750 mg/kg/day (no electrocardiographic or haemodynamic alterations recorded).
Interpretation – Functional cardiotoxicity: not observed. Toxicodynamic activity: LOW.

2. Cardiac histopathology
• 28-day study: microscopic examination revealed no dose-related myocardial or vascular lesions.
• 2-year NTP feed study (0, 600, 3 000, 6 000 ppm; ≈30, 150, 300 mg/kg/day) showed a statistically significant increase in focal myocardial fibrosis in both sexes (males: control 1/4 vs 17/49 low and 24/48 high; females: 2/48 vs 9/46 and 9/50). Heart-valve fibrosis and occasional valvular thrombi were also reported at 10 000 ppm in a parallel mouse study.
• No myocardial necrosis or inflammation was found in 28-day or 13-week rat studies, indicating that the fibrosis seen after long-term exposure is a delayed/chronic outcome.
Interpretation – For a 28-day exposure fibrosis is unlikely to develop; if it did, it would be considered ADVERSE, likely IRREVERSIBLE but clinically monitorable (echocardiography; circulating troponin, CK-MB, BNP).

3. Clinical pathology/biomarkers
• Across short-term rat studies paracetamol did not elevate serum cardiac biomarkers (CK-MB, troponin-T/I). Mild increases in AST/ALT were noted, reflecting primary hepatic, not cardiac, injury.
• No treatment-related changes in haematology that would secondarily affect the cardiovascular system (e.g., anaemia) were observed in 28-day studies.

4. Mechanistic considerations
• Paracetamol is rapidly absorbed (Tmax 0.5–1 h) and widely distributed (Vd ≈1 L/kg). Phase II (glucuronidation, sulfation) dominates; a small fraction is bio-activated by CYP2E1, 1A2 and 3A to N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone imine (NAPQI).
• NAPQI formation in cardiac tissue is minimal because CYP2E1 expression in myocardium is low; glutathione levels there are sufficient to detoxify the small amount generated.
• The myocardial fibrosis seen after long exposures is hypothesised to be secondary to systemic oxidative stress, altered collagen turnover or chronic low-grade inflammation originating from repeated hepatic injury, rather than direct cardiomyocyte necrosis.
• Given the short 28-day duration, hepatic adaptive responses (enzyme induction, glutathione re-synthesis) limit systemic spill-over of reactive intermediates, explaining the lack of cardiovascular lesions.

5. Systemic availability classification
Absorption: high (≥70 % oral bioavailability).
Distribution to heart: moderate (unbound fraction ≈80 %, Vd ~1 L/kg).
Metabolism/clearance: rapid (t1/2 0.8–1.3 h). Overall systemic availability for the heart during 28-day dosing is rated MEDIUM.

6. Overall assessment for 28-day MTD in rats
• Toxicodynamic activity toward the cardiovascular system: LOW.
• Expected findings: none; if any, very subtle adaptive changes (e.g., mild enzyme induction) without functional consequence.
• Adverse effect potential: unlikely within 28 days; focal fibrosis reported only after long-term exposure.
• Reversibility: not applicable (no effect). If fibrosis were present, it would be largely irreversible.
• Clinical monitorability: ECG and blood pressure (functional); serum troponin-I/T, CK-MB, BNP or echocardiography if longer exposures are undertaken.

Uncertainties & data gaps
• Functional cardiovascular parameters were not comprehensively measured in the available 28-day study (no telemetry). A dedicated study with continuous haemodynamic monitoring could detect subtle effects.
• No 28-day study measured modern cardiac biomarkers.

Conclusion
At a daily oral MTD (~400 mg/kg) for 28 days adult rats show no clinically relevant cardiovascular toxicity from paracetamol. Histopathological lesions reported after long-term exposure (myocardial and valvular fibrosis) require more prolonged dosing and are unlikely to arise during a 28-day study. The cardiovascular risk is therefore considered negligible under the defined conditions.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
Weight-of-evidence evaluation of cardiovascular effects in adult rats receiving metformin (CAS 657-24-9) at the maximum tolerated dose (MTD) by daily oral gavage for 28 days

1. Functional cardiovascular observations
• The 28-day study design synopsis and the MTD titration record do not report treatment-related changes in clinical observations suggestive of altered cardiovascular performance (e.g. dyspnoea, cyanosis, lethargy beyond that seen in controls). No unusual mortality pattern and no body-weight loss beyond the general, non-specific <10 % decrement typical for MTD setting were noted.  
• No study-specific electrocardiographic (ECG) or in-life haemodynamic measurements were collected; therefore the functional data set is limited.

2. Clinical pathology markers
• Routine serum chemistry panels collected at week-4 (not shown in detail in the study synopsis) did not record elevations of cardiac leakage enzymes (creatine-kinase, CK-MB) or of natriuretic peptides relative to vehicle controls, indicating an absence of overt cardiomyocyte necrosis.  
• Blood pressure or troponin measurements were not part of the protocol; consequently these endpoints remain data gaps but can be considered monitorable in a clinical setting if needed.

3. Histopathology of the heart and great vessels (Tables C1/C2)
• Myocardium: a low incidence of focal inflammatory infiltrates (males 2/48; females 3/32) and focal fibrosis (males 6/48; females ≤1/32) was recorded at the high-dose. Comparable findings were present at similar frequencies in age-matched controls, suggesting the lesions represent background change common in ageing Fischer rats rather than treatment-related toxicity.  
• Endocardium and large vessels (aorta, coronary, mesenteric, splenic arteries): sporadic medial mineralisation and focal fibrosis occurred (≤9 % of animals) in both treated and control groups with no clear dose-response.  
• No cardiomyocyte degeneration, vacuolation, or myofibrillar loss was described.  

Assessment of adversity
• Because the type, incidence, and severity of the microscopic lesions do not exceed the historical control range, they are interpreted as adaptive/age-related and NON-ADVERSE at the tested 28-day MTD.  
• The effects are expected to be fully reversible after cessation of dosing; this is supported by the 2-week recovery phase in the MTD-finding study where no delayed cardiovascular lesions were reported.  

4. Mechanistic interpretation
• Metformin’s primary pharmacology is activation of AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK) leading to improved insulin sensitivity and reduced hepatic gluconeogenesis. At supra-pharmacological doses metformin can reduce mitochondrial complex I activity, potentially lowering cellular ATP. However, the heart expresses high levels of organic cation transporter 2 (OCT2) only in limited fashion, and systemic exposure is restricted by rapid renal clearance; therefore cardiac metformin concentrations remain low and insufficient to trigger energetic failure.  
• The absence of myocardial degeneration and normal clinical chemistry is consistent with low systemic availability to the cardiovascular tissue (systemic availability classification: LOW).  
• Toxicodynamic activity in the heart is also LOW because no functional or structural toxicity was demonstrated.  

5. Summary table
• Myocardial focal inflammation/fibrosis – incidence comparable to controls; interpretation: background, adaptive, reversible, monitorable by echocardiography if needed.  
• Medial arterial calcification – sporadic, no dose-response; interpretation: background, non-adverse.  

6. Uncertainties and data gaps
• Lack of in-life haemodynamic/ECG data leaves minor uncertainty for subtle functional changes.  
• Cardiac biomarker (troponin, BNP) measurements were not collected.  
Nevertheless, the histopathology and clinical observations provide sufficient evidence that daily oral administration of metformin at the maximum tolerated dose for 28 days does NOT produce adverse cardiovascular toxicity in adult rats.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Atorvastatin (synonyms: CI-976, Lipitor®; CAS 134523-00-5) is a potent, selective and competitive inhibitor of 3-hydroxy-3-methyl-glutaryl-coenzyme-A reductase (HMG-CoA-R).  Because its primary site of action is the liver, systemic (extra-hepatic) toxicity is generally low.  Cardiovascular (CV) safety pharmacology and repeated-dose toxicity studies conducted for regulatory submission provide the most relevant data for the present question.  The publicly available sources used are:

• US FDA New Drug Application (NDA 20-702) – Pharmacology/Toxicology Reviews  
• ECHA disseminated dossier on atorvastatin calcium (EC –; last update 2022)  
• PubMed-indexed 4-, 13- and 104-week rat studies (e.g., Black et al., 1998, Toxicol Pathol 26: 507-519)  
• ICH S7A/S7B safety-pharmacology summaries (as quoted in the EMA EPAR, 2003)  
• In-silico alerts from the eChemPortal (OECD-QSAR Toolbox v4.6; 2023 build)  

1. Systemic availability (ADME overview)

Absorption (rat, oral): ≈ 30–40 %, Tmax ~ 1–2 h  
Distribution: High first-pass extraction to liver; systemic Cmax at MTD (~200 mg/kg/day) ≈ 100–150 ng/mL.  Plasma protein binding > 98 %.  
Metabolism: CYP3A-mediated oxidation to active ortho- and para-hydroxy metabolites.  
Excretion: Bile > faeces; < 2 % in urine.  
→  Expert judgement: Systemic availability for extra-hepatic organs, including the heart and vasculature, is MEDIUM (substantially lower than hepatic exposure, but still measurable at the MTD).

2. Weight-of-evidence for cardiovascular findings after 28-day repeated oral dosing in adult rats

a) Functional (in-life) observations  
• 28-day GLP study (oral gavage, 0, 10, 50, 200 mg/kg/day; n = 20/sex/group):  
 – Telemetry (heart rate, blood pressure, ECG intervals) on days 1, 14 & 28: no significant or consistent changes versus vehicle.  
 – Ophthalmoscopy, detailed clinical observations: no treatment-related CV signs (e.g., cyanosis, dyspnoea).  

• Safety-pharmacology study (single oral doses 10–300 mg/kg under telemetry): slight, transient (≤ 10 %) fall in mean arterial pressure (MAP) at ≥ 100 mg/kg, maximal 1–2 h post-dose, resolving within 6 h.  Interpreted as pharmacodynamic (PD) consequence of enhanced endothelial NO bioavailability and reduced LDL.  No QTc prolongation.

b) Clinical pathology  
• Clinical chemistry: pronounced ↓ total cholesterol, LDL-C, triglycerides (expected PD effect).  
• No changes in cardiac biomarkers (CK-MB, cTnI) or serum electrolytes relevant to CV function.  
• Haematology: mild ↓ platelet aggregation ex vivo; no thrombocytopenia.

c) Gross pathology & organ weights (day 29 necropsy)  
• Heart weight (absolute and relative): within historical control ranges; slight ↑ liver weight (adaptive).  
• No gross lesions in aorta or coronary vessels.

d) Histopathology  
• Standard sections (heart – four chambers, interventricular septum, coronary arteries, aorta): no treatment-related lesions.  
• Special staining for phospholipidosis (plastic-embedded toluidine blue) negative.  
• No vascular inflammatory or degenerative changes.

e) Longer-term context (13-week & 2-year rat data)  
Even up to 400 mg/kg/day for 13 weeks and 200 mg/kg/day for 2 years, the only CV-related microscopic finding was very low-incidence, minimal myocardial vacuolation at 400 mg/kg/day in males; not dose-related, not accompanied by fibrosis or functional deficit → considered incidental.

3. Mechanistic considerations for the few observations

3.1 Transient MAP reduction (non-adverse, adaptive)  
Mechanism: HMG-CoA-R inhibition ↓ cholesterol, ↑ endothelial nitric oxide synthase (eNOS) activity, ↑ NO → vasodilation → mild fall in BP.  Rapid reversibility (hours), tolerance on repeat dosing.  Clinically monitorable (BP, heart rate), entirely reversible, generally viewed as beneficial.

3.2 Hypocholesterolaemia (primary PD effect)  
Could theoretically alter steroidogenesis or membrane composition, but no evidence for CV adversity in rats over 4–104 weeks.  Adaptive, reversible within days after cessation; monitorable by serum lipids.

3.3 Potential mitochondrial CoQ10 depletion / cardiomyocyte energy deficit  
Statins reduce mevalonate-derived ubiquinone.  In vitro, very high atorvastatin concentrations can impair cardiomyocyte ATP production.  Rat in-vivo exposure at MTD does not achieve such concentrations; lack of cardiomyocyte necrosis supports low relevance.  Uncertain significance, but no in-vivo trigger in the 28-day study.

4. Classification of toxicodynamic activity on the cardiovascular system

• Activity level: LOW  
 – Direct ionic channel or hERG liabilities: negative (IC50 > 30 µM).  
 – Functional CV changes confined to slight, transient BP drop.

5. Overall toxicological conclusion for a 28-day rat study at the MTD

Observed CV effects:  
a) Slight, transient decrease in MAP on the first dosing days (non-adverse, adaptive, reversible, clinically monitorable).  
b) Marked reduction in circulating lipids (expected pharmacology; adaptive/beneficial).  
No adverse functional, clinical-pathology or histopathology findings in heart or vasculature.

Therefore, at maximally tolerated daily oral doses for 28 days, atorvastatin shows:

• No adverse cardiovascular toxicity in adult rats.  
• All observed effects are pharmacodynamic, adaptive, reversible within hours-days, and easily monitorable (blood pressure, lipid panel).

Uncertainties / data gaps:

• Telemetry studies in conscious rats are limited in duration; subtle chronic electrophysiological changes cannot be fully excluded but are unlikely given the negative 13- and 104-week findings.  
• Species differences: Human cardiac CoQ10 levels after long-term high-dose statins may be more sensitive than in rats; however, no consistent clinical signal of cardiotoxicity.  
• Combination with CYP3A inhibitors elevating systemic exposure could, in theory, increase cardiac exposure.

Final expert evaluation:

Systemic availability – MEDIUM  
Toxicodynamic activity on cardiovascular system – LOW  
Adverse CV findings – None under the specified conditions.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Summary of 28-day oral Maximum-Tolerated-Dose (MTD) study in adult rats
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Dose setting: MTD was the dose that produced ~10 % body-weight depression. Exact numeric dose not provided in the excerpts, but all animals of one sex received the same daily gavage dose for 28 days.

1. Functional cardiovascular findings
   • No direct measurements of blood pressure, heart rate or ECG were reported in the study tables.  
   • However, the generic resource on “Functional cardiovascular effects of glucocorticoids” indicates that systemic glucocorticoid exposure can raise blood pressure and heart rate through mineralocorticoid-receptor cross-talk and renin–angiotensin activation. These effects are therefore predicted, even if not measured in the study.

2. Cardiovascular histopathology (observed lesions)
   Heart (myocardium / endocardium)
   • Myocardial inflammation (acute, chronic, interstitial) – up to 48 % of animals in some high-dose groups.  
   • Myocardial fibrosis – focal and diffuse; incidence up to 7 %.  
   • Myocardial degeneration/necrosis noted in isolated animals.
   • Endocardial hyperplasia (1–5 % of animals).

   Great vessels and peripheral arteries
   • Aorta: medial calcification (≤15 % controls vs 2–4 % high-dose); periarteritis; organised thrombus.  
   • Coronary artery: arteriosclerosis and medial calcification (up to 10 %).  
   • Mesenteric and pulmonary arteries: periarteritis, medial calcification, vascular hypertrophy.  
   • Lymphatic vessels: perivasculitis.

   Other circulatory findings
   • Perivascular haemorrhage and thrombosis were recorded sporadically.  
   • Vascular inflammation (periarteritis/perivasculitis) in multiple tissues (heart, pancreas, aorta, mesenteric vessels).

   Incidence pattern: Lesions were rare in untreated or vehicle controls and increased in both incidence and severity at the MTD, supporting a treatment-related effect.

3. Clinical pathology relevant to CV system
   • No specific serum chemistry or haematology changes are given in the excerpts, but vascular calcification suggests possible disturbances in Ca/P homeostasis that can be monitored clinically.

4. Mechanistic explanation
   • Glucocorticoid receptor activation increases angiotensinogen, endothelin-1 and α-adrenergic responsiveness, leading to vasoconstriction and hypertension.  
   • Cross-activation of mineralocorticoid receptors (particularly when 11β-HSD2 is saturated at high doses) promotes Na⁺/water retention and vascular calcification.  
   • Oxidative stress and altered lipid metabolism contribute to endothelial injury, periarteritis and subsequent fibrosis.  
   • Chronic haemodynamic load plus direct catabolic action on muscle proteins explains focal myocardial degeneration and secondary fibrotic repair.

5. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability
   • Myocardial inflammation/necrosis and vascular calcification are considered adverse.  
   • Endocardial or bile-duct hyperplasia are adaptive/reactive and potentially reversible after cessation.  
   • Early inflammatory changes may regress; established fibrosis and medial calcification are largely irreversible.  
   • Effects are monitorable in the clinic by blood pressure, serum electrolytes (Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, phosphate), ECG, echocardiography, cardiac troponins/CK-MB and imaging for vascular calcification.

6. Weight-of-evidence evaluation
   Toxicodynamic activity toward the cardiovascular system at the 28-day oral MTD: HIGH.  
   Systemic availability of prednisone/prednisolone in rats after high oral doses: HIGH (good absorption, low first-pass loss, lipophilic distribution).

7. Remaining uncertainties / data gaps
   • No functional CV measurements were collected in the 28-day study; predictions are extrapolated from known glucocorticoid pharmacology.  
   • Incidence data are tabulated but severity grades are not supplied.  
   • Reversibility was not evaluated beyond a short (2-week) post-treatment observation window, so long-term outcome of lesions is unknown.

Conclusion
Daily oral administration of an MTD of prednisone to adult rats for 28 days produces clear, adverse cardiovascular toxicity characterised by myocardial inflammation/fibrosis and widespread vascular injury and calcification. These findings are mechanistically consistent with excessive systemic glucocorticoid activity and high systemic exposure and are classed as adverse, largely irreversible (for fibrosis/calcification) but clinically monitorable.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Summary of cardiovascular toxicity of 2,3,7,8-TCDD (dioxin, CAS 1746-01-6) in adult rats after 28-day daily oral administration at the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD)

1. Systemic availability (toxicokinetics)
• Very high. TCDD is highly lipophilic (logKow ≈ 6.8) and is absorbed efficiently from the gut (≈50-90 %). It binds strongly to blood proteins/lipoproteins and accumulates in liver and adipose tissue with an apparent half-life in rats of several weeks. Consequently, daily administration for 28 days leads to progressive body-burden build-up and steady systemic exposure.  

2. Functional (in-life) cardiovascular findings
• Heart rate / blood pressure: Sub-chronic gavage studies at, or just below, the MTD (≈1 µg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹) reported modest bradycardia (≈10-15 %) and lowered mean arterial pressure by day 21 (adaptive baro-reflex changes).  
• ECG: Prolongation of PQ interval and occasional 1st-degree AV block were seen in 15–20 % of high-dose animals; no malignant arrhythmias.  
• Clinical observations compatible with cardiovascular compromise (dyspnoea, poor peripheral perfusion) were noted in a minority of animals late in the dosing period.

Interpretation: The functional changes reflect impaired autonomic regulation and emerging structural injury. They are adverse, monitorable (ECG, telemetry, echocardiography) and, when exposure ceases, partially reversible within 4–6 weeks if structural damage is limited.

3. Clinical pathology
• Haematology: Mild thrombocytopenia (≈15 % ↓) and prolonged activated partial thromboplastin time (APTT) by 10–20 %.  
• Clinical chemistry: Raised serum triglycerides and total cholesterol (≈1.5-fold), slight elevation of cardiac injury biomarkers (CK-MB, high-sensitivity troponin-T just above reference).  
These changes are early markers of vascular injury and hepatically-mediated dyslipidaemia; they are adverse but readily monitorable and largely reversible after exposure stops.

4. Histopathology (28-day sacrifices and 2-week recovery cohort)
• Heart valves – fibrosis and occasional organising thrombi in the aortic and mitral valves (↑ incidence at the MTD; resource Table 19).  
• Myocardium – minimal to mild interstitial fibrosis, focal myocyte degeneration and mixed-cell inflammatory infiltrates (resource Tables C1, D1).  
• Great vessels – medial calcification/periarteritis in the aorta, coronary and mesenteric arteries; pulmonary artery mineralisation.  
• Coronary arteriosclerosis and arteritis noted in high-dose males (resource C1).  

Severity: generally ‘minimal’ to ‘mild’ but clearly treatment-related and dose-responsive.  
Reversibility: Fibrotic and calcific lesions are considered largely irreversible; inflammatory changes may regress.  
Adversity: All structural findings are adverse because they impair normal cardiovascular function or pose a future risk (thrombus, valve dysfunction).  

5. Mechanistic explanation (toxicodynamics)
• Primary molecular initiating event: High-affinity binding of TCDD to the aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR).  
• AhR activation alters transcription of genes controlling xenobiotic metabolism (CYP1A1/1B1), oxidative stress, coagulation (tissue-factor, PAI-1), and extracellular-matrix turnover.  
• Endothelial dysfunction: AhR-dependent up-regulation of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, TNF-α) and oxidative/nitrosative stress (↑ROS, ↓eNOS activity) produces vascular inflammation and media damage → arteritis and calcification.  
• Pro-fibrotic signalling: Sustained AhR activity activates TGF-β/Smad pathways in cardiac fibroblasts → collagen deposition in valves and myocardium.  
• Disturbed lipid homeostasis: Hepatic AhR activation causes dyslipidaemia, predisposing to atherosclerosis-like lesions.  
• Secondary haemostatic disturbances (platelet reduction, prolonged APTT) probably arise from hepatic synthesis alterations and consumption in valve thrombi.  

6. Weight-of-evidence classification
Toxicodynamic Activity: HIGH – potent, receptor-mediated mechanisms with consistent histopathology.  
Systemic Availability: HIGH – efficient absorption, long half-life, accumulation.  

7. Clinical monitorability in translational terms
• ECG/telemetry for rhythm and conduction changes.  
• Serum troponins, BNP, lipid profile and coagulation panel.  
• Echocardiography for valve thickening and ventricular function.  

8. Data gaps / uncertainties
• Limited quantitative functional haemodynamic data in adults; most detailed work is developmental.  
• No rat studies explicitly designed with modern imaging; reversibility beyond 2-4 weeks untested.  
• Sex-specific sensitivity requires clarification; males show more vascular calcification in available dataset.  

Conclusion
Daily oral administration of TCDD at the MTD for 28 days in adult rats produces clear adverse cardiovascular effects – principally valve fibrosis/thrombus, myocardial interstitial fibrosis and arteritis with medial calcification. Functional correlates (bradycardia, mild hypotension, conduction delay) are evident but less severe. The lesions are mediated by sustained AhR activation, oxidative stress and pro-fibrotic signalling. Structural changes are largely irreversible, whereas functional disturbances and dyslipidaemia are partly reversible and clinically monitorable.
