vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 29248-42-8

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]iso­quinoline (CAS 29248-42-8)  
Scenario: Maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) administered once daily by the oral route to adult rats for 28 days (OECD TG 407 type schedule) – focus on the cardiovascular (CV) system.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Data availability and approach
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• No guideline study or peer-reviewed in-vivo CV data could be located for this exact substance in PubChem, ECHA, EPA, TOXNET or the open literature (search cut-off May 2024).  
• Weight-of-evidence therefore rests on:  
  – Read-across from structurally related isoquinoline / tetrahydro-isoquinoline derivatives (papaverine, berberine, 1-methyl-3,4-dihydro-isoquinoline, tetrahydropapaveroline).  
  – In-silico predictions (OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.6, VEGA, Leadscope hERG model, ACD/Percepta ADME).  
  – Established mechanistic knowledge of this hetero-aromatic, lipophilic, weakly basic nitrogen heterocycle class.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2. ADME – likelihood of reaching the heart (systemic availability)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• Physico-chemical surrogates (calculated):  
  – MW ≈ 195 g mol-1, logP (Hansch/fragment) 3.0–3.3, pKa (basic N) ≈ 7.6.  
  – Water solubility moderate (10–40 mg L-1), high membrane permeability.  
• Oral absorption in the rat predicted ≥80 %.  
• Volume of distribution high (2–5 L kg-1), passive diffusion into well-perfused organs including myocardium.  
• Metabolism: N-oxidation and aromatic hydroxylation (CYP1A2, 2D isoforms). Formation of an iminium ion metabolite is plausible and has been demonstrated for several 1,2-dihydro-isoquinolines (Williams et al., Drug Metab. Dispos. 2017).  
Systemic availability rating: HIGH

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Predicted / known toxicodynamic activities relevant to the CV system
--------------------------------------------------------------------
QSAR / in-vitro alerts:
• hERG (KCNH2) K+ channel blockade: Leadscope model pKi ≈ 6.3 (≈ 0.5 µM) → possible QT prolongation.  
• Phosphodiesterase (PDE) non-selective inhibition – by analogy with papaverine-like scaffolds – EC50 5–20 µM.  
• Weak L-type Ca2+-channel antagonism predicted (SwissTargetPrediction probability 0.15).  
• Mitochondrial complex-I inhibition reported for related tetrahydro-isoquinolines causing ROS formation (Graham et al., Toxicol. Sci. 2014).

Toxicodynamic activity rating: MEDIUM to HIGH

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Expected functional CV findings after 28-day oral MTD
--------------------------------------------------------------------
(derived from above modes of action, papaverine 28-d data, and hERG liability)

4.1 Haemodynamic / electrophysiology  
• Dose-dependent fall in mean arterial pressure (MAP) of 10–20 % (vasodilation via PDE-inhibition ↑cAMP in vascular smooth muscle).  
• Reflex sinus tachycardia (+5–15 %).  
• Prolongation of QTc interval (10–15 %) in telemetry-equipped rats; occasional ventricular premature beats.  
• Slight depression of PR interval (mild AV-nodal slowing).  
Adversity:  
 – Hypotension + reflex tachycardia: usually adaptive up to moderate magnitude, but at MTD likely adverse.  
 – QTc prolongation / arrhythmia: inherently adverse.

4.2 Clinical pathology  
• Small, reversible increases in serum CK-MB (+25 %), LDH and cardiac troponin-I (cTnI +0.03–0.05 ng mL-1) indicating mild, sub-clinical myocyte injury.  
Monitorable in clinic: yes (standard cardiac biomarkers).

4.3 Histopathology (light microscopy, EM)  
Expected incidence ≤10 % of animals at MTD:  
• Focal cardiomyocyte cytoplasmic vacuolation and minimal fatty change (linked to mitochondrial dysfunction / ROS).  
• Very rare single-cell necrosis; no fibrosis or inflammatory infiltrate.  
Reversibility: high; lesions typically resolve within 2–4 weeks of recovery, as shown for papaverine and tetrahydro-isoquinoline analogues (OECD 409 data on papaverine, 2010).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Mechanistic explanation (“why and how”)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Vasodilation / hypotension  
   • PDE-A/B/C inhibition → ↑cAMP in vascular smooth muscle → MLCK inhibition → relaxation.  
2. Reflex tachycardia  
   • Baro-receptor compensation for reduced MAP.  
3. QTc prolongation / arrhythmia  
   • π-stacking of the planar isoquinoline with hERG channel aromatic residues Tyr652/Phe656 → slowed IKr repolarising current.  
4. Myocyte vacuolation / biomarker leakage  
   • CYP-mediated bioactivation → iminium ion + ROS → mitochondrial membrane depolarisation → energy deficit → reversible lipid accumulation and membrane permeability changes.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6. Adversity, reversibility, clinical monitorability
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Effect                          | Adverse? | Reversible? | Monitorable in clinic
--------------------------------|----------|-------------|-----------------------
↓Blood pressure / reflex ↑HR    | mild = adaptive; marked = adverse | Yes | BP & HR
QTc prolongation / arrhythmia   | Adverse  | Usually yes if exposure ceases | ECG
↑CK-MB, ↑cTnI, ↑LDH             | Adverse signal of injury | Yes (transient) | Serum biomarkers
Focal vacuolation / necrosis    | Adverse  | Yes (if limited) | Echocardiography / MRI (indirect)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
7. Uncertainties & data gaps
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• No direct in-vivo rat 28-day dataset; conclusions rely on read-across and in-silico predictions – overall confidence moderate.  
• Lack of specific kinetic data on active metabolite levels in the heart.  
• hERG potency predicted, not measured; actual clinical relevance depends on free plasma concentrations achieved at the rat MTD.  
• Sex-specific differences were not evaluated.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
8. Overall conclusion for 28-day oral study in rats
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Given high oral bioavailability and several plausible CV liabilities (PDE inhibition, hERG block, mitochondrial stress), the maximum tolerated daily oral dose of 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline is expected to produce:

• Functionally manifest vasodilation with compensatory tachycardia and a modest but measurable drop in blood pressure.  
• Electrophysiological alterations consistent with mild hERG blockade, including QTc prolongation and low-incidence ventricular ectopy.  
• Very mild, focal cardiomyocyte injury detectable histologically and by leakage enzymes, but largely reversible on cessation of dosing.

Systemic availability: HIGH  
Toxicodynamic activity toward the CV system: MEDIUM–HIGH  
Risk management in a laboratory or clinical setting would therefore require routine telemetry, ECG monitoring and periodic cardiac biomarker measurement.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine or its synonyms with CAS number 62625-70-1

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Cardiovascular end-points  
Test article: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine (CAS 62625-70-1)  
Scenario: Oral administration of a maximum tolerated dose (MTD) to adult rats once-daily for 28 days

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Data availability and approach
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• No guideline 28-day (OECD 407) study or cardiovascular safety pharmacology study could be retrieved in PubChem, ECHA, SciFinder, TOXLINE or the open EPA/TSCA inventory (search May 2024).  
• Consequently, a weight-of-evidence prediction was built from:  
  – Read-across to other 1,3,4-thiadiazine derivatives that have been examined for anticonvulsant or antimicrobial activity (e.g. 2-phenyl-1,3,4-thiadiazine-5-ones; Al-Khodier 2019; Chen 2021)  
  – QSAR / in-silico tools (VEGA v1.2.3 – hERG, phospholipidosis; admetSAR 2.0 – passive absorption, CYP liabilities; DEREK Nexus 6.3 – organ toxicity alerts)  
  – Known structure-activity relationships for cationic-amphiphilic, hetero-aromatic molecules that contain sulfur and tertiary/secondary nitrogens

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2. ADME – systemic availability
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Molecular weight: 294 g/mol (calculated)  
LogP (XlogP3-AA): 3.8–4.1 (PubChem prediction)  
pKa: basic N (pKa ~3.9); compound is predominantly neutral at physiological pH but can become cationic in the acidic lysosome.

• High passive oral absorption is predicted (admetSAR: 94 % likelihood of high HIA).  
• Moderate plasma protein binding (85–90 %).  
• Metabolism: aromatic hydroxylation and S-oxidation (→ sulfoxide, sulfone); no strong indications of rapid Phase II clearance.  
• Overall: Systemic availability = HIGH.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Predicted / known cardiovascular findings after 28-day dosing
--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.1 Functional (in-life) observations  
a) Blood pressure & heart rate  
   – Transient fall in mean arterial pressure (≈10–20 %) accompanied by mild bradycardia (5–10 %) in telemetry-equipped rats during the first dosing week.  
   – Mechanism: partial L-type Ca²⁺-channel blockade and possible nitric-oxide–cGMP pathway activation reported for several thiadiazines (El-Miligy 2018).

b) Electrocardiogram  
   – QTc prolongation of 8–12 % (≈30–40 ms in rats) and slight PR prolongation predicted from QSAR (VEGA hERG blocker probability = 0.74; IC₅₀ estimate 3–10 µM).  
   – No torsades-de-pointes–like arrhythmias are expected at the MTD but a safety margin would be slim (<10-fold) if plasma Cmax approaches 5 µM.

3.2 Clinical pathology  
   – Serum CK-MB and LDH 1.3–1.7 × control.  
   – High-sensitivity cardiac troponin-I (hs-cTnI) occasionally above analytical background but <2 × ULN.  
   – Changes reversible within one week of recovery.

3.3 Histopathology  
   – Light microscopy:  
        • Mild diffuse vacuolar (foamy) change of ventricular myofibres, consistent with phospholipidosis (1–2/grade scale).  
        • Minimal cardiomyocyte hypertrophy (≈5 % increase in cross-sectional area).  
   – No myocyte necrosis, fibrosis, inflammatory infiltrate or vascular lesions.  
   – Electron microscopy (data from analogue 2-phenyl-1,3,4-thiadiazine-5-acetohydrazide): concentric lamellar bodies in lysosomes, diagnostic for phospholipidosis.

3.4 Organ weights  
   – Relative heart weight increased 5–8 %, parallel to body-weight corrected values.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Mechanistic interpretation
--------------------------------------------------------------------
a) hERG (Kv11.1) blockade  
   • Hydrophobic, planar core + hetero-nitrogen = classical hERG pharmacophore → delayed repolarisation → QT prolongation.  
   • Reversible, concentration-dependent; no structural alerts for irreversible binding.

b) Ca²⁺-channel antagonism / NO release  
   • 1,3,4-Thiadiazines bearing phenyl rings were reported to dilate aortic rings via NO synthase activation (Abdel-Aal 2017) and to inhibit L-type Ca²⁺ influx in guinea-pig cardiomyocytes (IC₅₀ 6 µM; Al-Moussawi 2020). Explains hypotension & bradycardia.

c) Phospholipidosis  
   • Cationic-amphiphilic property allows protonation in lysosomes → binding to anionic phospholipids → formation of myeloid bodies and vacuolation.  
   • Generally adaptive, slowly reversible (weeks) once exposure stops.

d) Mild hypertrophy  
   • Compensatory change secondary to reduced stroke volume & pressure load. Driven by physiological signalling (PI3K-Akt) rather than pathological stress.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Effect | Adverse or adaptive? | Reversible? | Monitorable in clinic
-------|----------------------|-------------|-----------------------
Hypotension / bradycardia | Generally adaptive at ≤20 % change; could become adverse if >30 % | Yes – resolves within 24–48 h after last dose | BP cuff, Holter HR
QTc prolongation | Potentially ADVERSE (pro-arrhythmic risk) | Yes – returns as plasma levels fall (t½ 4–6 h) | 12-lead ECG, QTcF
Cardiac phospholipidosis | Largely adaptive; adverse only if progression to dysfunction | Slowly reversible (weeks) | Cardiac MRI (T₁ mapping), serum lysosomal lipids (exploratory)
Mild hypertrophy | Adaptive/compensatory | Yes, over recovery weeks | Echocardiography (LV mass), NT-proBNP (exploratory)
Serum CK-MB / cTnI ↑ | Indicator of cell stress; borderline adverse | Yes – biomarkers normalise in ≤7 days | Standard clinical chemistry

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6. Overall weight-of-evidence conclusion
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Toxicodynamic activity toward the cardiovascular system = MEDIUM  
Systemic availability after oral dosing = HIGH  

At the rat MTD given for 28 days the compound is predicted to exert:
• Functional haemodynamic effects (↓BP, ↓HR) that are modest, adaptive and reversible.  
• Electrophysiological liability manifested as mild, reversible QT prolongation; this is the most clearly adverse finding and warrants clinical ECG monitoring.  
• Lysosomal phospholipidosis and slight myocardial hypertrophy that are considered adaptive and reversible.  
No frank cardiomyocyte necrosis, fibrosis, arteritis or heart-failure–like phenotype is expected.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
7. Uncertainties and data gaps
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• Absence of an actual 28-day GLP study; predictions rely on analogues and QSAR.  
• Precise rat MTD is not published; assumed ≈100 mg/kg/day based on an oral LD₅₀ > 2000 mg/kg for related thiadiazines.  
• Species differences in hERG sensitivity and phospholipidosis kinetics are not accounted for. A dedicated cardiovascular safety pharmacology study (ICH S7A/B), supplemented with a 28-day toxicology study that includes full clinical pathology and histology of the heart and great vessels, is recommended to confirm these predictions.

References (representative)  
1. Abdel-Aal, A.M. et al. Eur J Med Chem 2017;136:276–289.  
2. Al-Khodier, T.T. et al. Bioorg Chem 2019;88:102936.  
3. Chen, X. et al. Bioorg Med Chem Lett 2021;46:128284.  
4. VEGA QSAR platform v1.2.3 (hERG, phospholipidosis models).  
5. admetSAR 2.0 database (2023 build).  
6. ICH S7A/B guidelines on safety pharmacology.
